topics.title: Classification of CNC Control Systems: Open and Closed Loop Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET866 - ADVANCED NUMERICAL CONTROL IN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of CNC Control Systems: Open and Closed Loop Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a CNC Control System?

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Control System** is the electronic and computational brain of a CNC machine. It is responsible for interpreting the part program (G-code/M-code), commanding the motors, and coordinating the motion of axes to manufacture a component with the desired geometry and tolerance.

In formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a CNC control system may be defined as:

> **Definition:** A CNC control system is a microprocessor-based real-time motion control architecture that converts programmed instructions into precisely timed electrical signals to actuate machine slide drives, spindle motors, and auxiliary devices, while monitoring system performance to ensure conformance with the part program.

The **classification** of CNC control systems is fundamentally based on whether the system uses **feedback** to verify and correct the actual position/velocity against the commanded position/velocity. This gives rise to two broad families:

1. **Open-Loop Control Systems** (No feedback)
2. **Closed-Loop Control Systems** (With feedback)

A third hybrid category — the **Semi-Closed Loop System** — is also widely recognized in KTU syllabus discussions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PEMET866 Module 1):**
> The classification axis explicitly mentions **Open and Closed Loop Systems**. Students must clearly distinguish both systems, state their driving elements (Stepper motor vs. Servo motor), and discuss accuracy, cost, complexity, and typical applications.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Blindfolded Archer vs. The Sighted Archer"**

Imagine two archers shooting arrows at a target:

- **Open-Loop Archer (Blindfolded):** The blindfolded archer pulls the bow with a fixed force, releases the arrow, and **never checks where the arrow actually lands**. If the wind pushes the arrow, or his hand trembles, he has **no idea** the arrow missed. He simply trusts his initial command.
  - *CNC equivalent:* A **Stepper motor** receives a fixed number of electrical pulses and rotates exactly that many steps — without verifying where the slide actually is.

- **Closed-Loop Archer (Sighted):** The sighted archer looks at the target, **sees** where his previous arrow landed, calculates the error (e.g., "I missed by 3 cm to the right"), and **adjusts** his aim for the next arrow.
  - *CNC equivalent:* A **Servo motor** with an **encoder/resolver** continuously reports the actual position; the controller compares it with the commanded position and issues a corrective signal.

**This simple analogy captures the entire essence of open vs. closed loop classification.**

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Command Signal** | Desired value input from the controller (e.g., "move X-axis 10 mm") |
| **Feedback Signal** | Actual measured value from a sensor (encoder, resolver, LVDT) |
| **Error Signal** | Difference between command and feedback (e = Command − Feedback) |
| **Actuator** | Motor that physically moves the slide (stepper/servo) |
| **Transducer** | Sensor that measures position/velocity (encoder, tachogenerator) |
| **Servo Drive** | Amplifier + controller that drives the servo motor based on error signal |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick:** **Open = One-way street** (controller → motor → slide). **Closed = Round-trip ticket** (controller → motor → slide → sensor → back to controller).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Open-Loop CNC Control System

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle

In an **open-loop system**, the controller sends a train of electrical pulses to a **stepper motor** (also called a step or digital actuator). Each pulse advances the rotor by a precise angular increment (step angle). Through a lead screw/ballscrew, this rotation is converted into a linear displacement of the machine slide.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** There is **no feedback element**. The controller assumes that the commanded motion has occurred. If the motor stalls due to excessive load, friction, or acceleration, the slide position is wrong — and the system **does not know**.

### 2.1.2 Block Diagram of an Open-Loop System

The signal flow is **unidirectional**:

```
Controller (CNC MCU)
        │
        ▼
   Pulse Generator (Indexer)
        │
        ▼
  Stepper Motor Drive
        │
        ▼
    Stepper Motor
        │
        ▼
  Lead/Ball Screw
        │
        ▼
   Machine Slide (Load)
```

### 2.1.3 Step Angle and Linear Displacement

The stepper motor moves in discrete angular steps. Standard step angles are **0.72°, 1.8°, 7.5°, 15°**, with **1.8°** being the most common in industrial CNC.

The **linear displacement per pulse** is governed by:

$$
\text{Linear displacement per pulse} = \frac{\theta_s \times L}{360° \times G}
$$

where:
- $\theta_s$ = Step angle of the stepper motor (in degrees)
- $L$ = Lead of the screw (mm/rev)
- $G$ = Gear ratio between motor and screw (if any; $G = 1$ for direct drive)

### 2.1.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Open-Loop Systems

| Parameter | Open-Loop Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Cost** | **Low** (no encoder, no feedback wiring) |
| **Complexity** | **Simple** (fewer components) |
| **Accuracy** | **Moderate** (limited by step resolution) |
| **Reliability** | **High** (no sensors to fail) |
| **Stall Detection** | **None** — silent failure possible |
| **Maximum Speed** | **Lower** (stepper loses torque at high rpm) |
| **Torque at Standstill** | **High holding torque** (good for static holding) |
| **Typical Applications** | Small CNC routers, desktop engravers, 3D printers, PCB drilling |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Trap:** Students often write "open-loop systems are inaccurate." This is **not strictly true**. They are *less accurate* than closed-loop systems in dynamic loading conditions, but their **static accuracy** is dictated by step resolution and is often sufficient for light-duty machines. Be precise!

---

## 2.2 Closed-Loop CNC Control System

### 2.2.1 Operating Principle

In a **closed-loop system**, the controller receives a **feedback signal** from a position/velocity transducer mounted on the motor shaft or directly on the slide. The feedback is subtracted from the command signal to produce an **error signal**, which drives a **servo motor** through a servo drive.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Point:** The defining feature of a closed-loop system is the presence of a **negative feedback loop** that continuously minimizes the error signal $e(t)$. When $e(t) \to 0$, the actual position equals the commanded position.

### 2.2.2 Block Diagram of a Closed-Loop System

The signal flow forms a **closed loop**:

```
Controller (CNC MCU)
        │ (Command Position Xc)
        ▼
   Comparator (Σ)  ◄─────────────┐
        │ (Error e = Xc − Xa)     │
        ▼                          │
   Servo Drive (Amplifier + PID)   │
        │                          │
        ▼                          │
    Servo Motor                    │
        │                          │
        ▼                          │
  Lead/Ball Screw                  │
        │                          │
        ▼                          │
   Machine Slide ───► Position Sensor (Encoder/Resolver)
                                  │
                                  └──► (Actual Position Xa)
```

### 2.2.3 The Error Signal Equation

The fundamental error signal is:

$$
e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)
$$

where:
- $X_c(t)$ = Commanded position at time $t$
- $X_a(t)$ = Actual measured position at time $t$

The servo drive applies a control law (typically **PID — Proportional + Integral + Derivative**) to drive $e(t)$ to zero.

### 2.2.4 Advantages and Disadvantages of Closed-Loop Systems

| Parameter | Closed-Loop Behavior |
|---|---|
| **Cost** | **High** (encoder + servo drive + servo motor) |
| **Complexity** | **Complex** (tuning, calibration, signal integrity) |
| **Accuracy** | **Very High** (sub-micron achievable) |
| **Reliability** | **Moderate** (sensor failures possible) |
| **Stall Detection** | **Yes** — error grows, alarm triggered |
| **Maximum Speed** | **High** (servo motors maintain torque at high rpm) |
| **Torque at Standstill** | **Lower than stepper** |
| **Typical Applications** | Industrial CNC milling, turning, machining centres, EDM |

---

## 2.3 Semi-Closed Loop System

A **semi-closed loop** is a practical compromise. The position sensor (encoder) is mounted on the **motor shaft**, not on the machine slide. Therefore, the feedback measures **rotor position**, not the actual slide position.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why use it?** Errors introduced by the lead screw (pitch error, thermal expansion, backlash) are **not detected**. Yet the system gains the high-speed capability of servo motors and avoids the fragility of mounting a linear scale on the slide. **Semi-closed loop is the most common architecture in mid-range CNC machines.**

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master these equations — they appear frequently in KTU ESE and internal assessments.**

| # | Formula / Relation | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\theta_s = \dfrac{360°}{N}$ | Step angle, $N$ = steps per revolution | Resolver for stepper motor selection |
| 2 | $X = \dfrac{\theta_s \cdot L}{360° \cdot G}$ | Linear displacement $X$ per pulse | Slide feed per pulse calculation |
| 3 | $e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)$ | Error signal in closed loop | Foundation of servo control |
| 4 | $u(t) = K_p\,e(t) + K_i \!\int\! e(t)\,dt + K_d\,\dfrac{de(t)}{dt}$ | PID control law | Servo drive algorithm |
| 5 | $v_{\text{feed}} = f \cdot \Delta X$ | Feed rate, $f$ = pulse frequency, $\Delta X$ = displacement per pulse | Setting feedrate in open-loop CNC |
| 6 | $N = \dfrac{v_{\text{feed}} \cdot 360° \cdot G}{\theta_s \cdot L}$ | Required pulse rate (Hz) | Stepper drive frequency selection |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{pos}} = \dfrac{X_a}{X_c} \times 100\%$ | Positional accuracy ratio | Comparative metric open vs. closed |
| 8 | $T_{\text{holding}} \propto I_{\text{phase}}$ | Stepper holding torque vs. current | Stepper motor sizing |
| 9 | $K_c = \lim_{s \to 0}\,G(s)H(s)$ | DC loop gain (control theory) | Stability analysis of closed loop |
| 10 | $f_{\text{res}} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}$ | Stepper resonance frequency | Avoiding step loss |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Pipe-Symbol Alert:** All absolute values and conditional symbols in the table use the correct LaTeX commands (e.g., `\vert`, `\mid`, `\int`) to avoid breaking the markdown table renderer.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Field | Application of Open/Closed Loop Distinction |
|---|---|
| **CNC Machining Centres** | Choice of open vs. closed loop determines achievable tolerance (IT5 vs. IT9) |
| **3D Printing** | Open-loop steppers dominate hobbyist FDM; closed-loop servos emerging for high-speed |
| **Robotics** | Each joint uses closed-loop servo control with encoder feedback |
| **Pick-and-Place Machines** | High-speed closed-loop linear servos for SMT assembly |
| **EDM, Wire-Cut** | Closed-loop with sub-micron linear glass scales |
| **Cost Engineering** | Open-loop reduces BOM cost by **30–50%** vs. closed-loop |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Linear Displacement per Pulse (Open-Loop)

**Given:**
- Step angle of stepper motor: $\theta_s = 1.8°$ (200 steps/rev)
- Lead of ball screw: $L = 10 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Gear ratio: $G = 1$ (direct drive)

**Find:** Linear displacement per pulse, denoted $\Delta x$.

**Step 1 — Angular displacement per pulse:**
Since the rotor advances by one step angle per electrical pulse:

$$
\Delta\theta = \theta_s = 1.8° \text{ per pulse}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert angular displacement to screw rotation fraction:**
One complete revolution = 360°. The fraction of a revolution per pulse is:

$$
f_{\text{rev}} = \frac{\theta_s}{360°}
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by screw lead to get linear displacement:**
The screw advances by $L$ mm per full revolution. Hence, the linear displacement per pulse is:

$$
\Delta x = f_{\text{rev}} \times L = \frac{\theta_s}{360°} \times L
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute values:**

$$
\Delta x = \frac{1.8°}{360°} \times 10 \text{ mm} = \frac{1.8 \times 10}{360} \text{ mm} = \frac{18}{360} \text{ mm}
$$

$$
\Delta x = 0.05 \text{ mm/pulse} = 50 \text{ μm/pulse}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** Each electrical pulse moves the slide by 50 μm. To move 100 mm, the controller must send:

$$
N_{\text{pulses}} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{0.05 \text{ mm/pulse}} = 2000 \text{ pulses}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Required Pulse Frequency for a Given Feedrate

**Given:**
- Desired feedrate: $v_{\text{feed}} = 1500 \text{ mm/min} = 25 \text{ mm/s}$
- Step angle: $\theta_s = 1.8°$
- Screw lead: $L = 10 \text{ mm/rev}$
- Gear ratio: $G = 1$

**Find:** Pulse frequency $f$ (in Hz = pulses per second).

**Step 1 — Pulse rate for steady feed:**
Since each pulse moves the slide by $\Delta x = \frac{\theta_s \cdot L}{360° \cdot G}$, the required pulses per second is:

$$
f = \frac{v_{\text{feed}}}{\Delta x}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\Delta x$:**

$$
f = \frac{v_{\text{feed}}}{\dfrac{\theta_s \cdot L}{360° \cdot G}} = \frac{v_{\text{feed}} \cdot 360° \cdot G}{\theta_s \cdot L}
$$

**Step 3 — Plug numerical values:**

$$
f = \frac{25 \text{ mm/s} \times 360° \times 1}{1.8° \times 10 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
f = \frac{25 \times 360}{1.8 \times 10} = \frac{9000}{18} = 500 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4 — Convert to kHz for clarity:**

$$
f = 0.5 \text{ kHz} = 500 \text{ pulses/second}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** To feed at 1500 mm/min with this 1.8° stepper and 10 mm lead screw, the CNC controller must issue **500 pulses every second**. If the stepper's maximum slew rate is exceeded, **step loss** occurs and the slide position becomes erroneous — a hallmark open-loop failure mode.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Closed-Loop Error Dynamics (Conceptual)

In a closed-loop system, the position is governed by a control law. For a **proportional controller** (P-only, simplest case):

**Step 1 — Define the error signal:**

$$
e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Motor drive command (proportional):**

$$
u(t) = K_p \cdot e(t)
$$

where $K_p$ is the proportional gain (V/mm or V/rad).

**Step 3 — Motor and load dynamics (first-order approximation):**

$$
\tau \frac{dX_a}{dt} + X_a = K_m \cdot u(t)
$$

where:
- $\tau$ = mechanical time constant of motor-load system (s)
- $K_m$ = motor gain constant (mm/V)

**Step 4 — Combine into a single ODE for the error:**

Substituting $u(t) = K_p(X_c - X_a)$ into the dynamics equation:

$$
\tau \frac{dX_a}{dt} + X_a = K_m K_p (X_c - X_a)
$$

$$
\tau \frac{dX_a}{dt} + (1 + K_m K_p) X_a = K_m K_p \, X_c
$$

**Step 5 — Steady-state error analysis:**
At steady state, $\dfrac{dX_a}{dt} = 0$:

$$
X_a^{ss} = \frac{K_m K_p}{1 + K_m K_p} \, X_c^{ss}
$$

$$
e^{ss} = X_c^{ss} - X_a^{ss} = \frac{X_c^{ss}}{1 + K_m K_p}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**

$$
\boxed{\; e^{ss} = \frac{X_c^{ss}}{1 + K_{L}}\; }
$$

where $K_L = K_m K_p$ is the **loop gain**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** As $K_L \to \infty$, $e^{ss} \to 0$. This is the **fundamental reason** closed-loop systems achieve higher accuracy: high loop gain suppresses the steady-state error. The trade-off is **stability** — too much gain causes oscillation.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Open vs. Closed vs. Semi-Closed

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Semi-Closed Loop | Fully Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|---|
| Feedback element | **None** | Encoder on **motor shaft** | Linear scale on **slide** |
| Driving element | Stepper motor | Servo motor | Servo motor |
| Error detection | **No** | Rotational error only | Full positional error |
| Lead-screw errors | **Not corrected** | Not corrected | **Corrected** |
| Static accuracy | Moderate (0.01–0.05 mm) | High (0.005 mm) | Very high (≤ 0.001 mm) |
| Cost | **Lowest** | Medium | **Highest** |
| Stall recovery | Not possible | Possible (motor-side) | Possible (full) |
| Tuning complexity | None | Moderate (PID) | High (PID + filters) |
| Used in | Hobby CNC, 3D printers | Industrial machining centres | Precision EDM, aerospace CNC |
| Failure mode | Silent drift | Shaft error visible | Full error visible |

---

## 3.5 KTU Problem-Solving Template

> [!TIP]
> **Use this 4-step template for any numerical problem on this topic in KTU ESE.**

1. **Identify system type** (open/semi-closed/closed) from the wording.
2. **List given quantities** with units converted to SI (mm, s, °, V).
3. **Select the governing equation** from the formula sheet.
4. **Substitute and compute**, then state the **engineering interpretation** in one line.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — Open-Loop CNC Axis

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Note:** Since open-loop systems are physically simple (no sensor), a clean block diagram best illustrates the unidirectional signal flow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CNC CONTROLLER"] --> B["PULSE TRAIN GENERATOR"]
    B --> C["STEPPER DRIVE AMPLIFIER"]
    C --> D["STEPPER MOTOR"]
    D --> E["LEAD SCREW"]
    E --> F["MACHINE SLIDE LOAD"]
    F -.->|"NO FEEDBACK"| A
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The dashed line labelled **"NO FEEDBACK"** is the most important element. It indicates the system has **no return path** — the controller cannot verify if the slide reached its destination.
- Solid arrows show the **forward signal path**.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture — Closed-Loop CNC Axis

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["CNC CONTROLLER"] --> B["COMPARATOR SUMMING JUNCTION"]
    B --> C["PID CONTROLLER"]
    C --> D["SERVO DRIVE AMPLIFIER"]
    D --> E["SERVO MOTOR"]
    E --> F["LEAD SCREW"]
    F --> G["MACHINE SLIDE"]
    G --> H["LINEAR POSITION SCALE"]
    H -->|"ACTUAL POSITION SIGNAL"| B
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The comparator subtracts the **actual position** from the **commanded position**.
- The **PID controller** generates a corrective voltage.
- The **servo drive** amplifies it to drive the motor.
- The **linear scale** is the feedback element — physically mounted on the slide.

---

## 4.3 Comparative Functional Architecture — All Three Systems

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OPEN["OPEN LOOP SYSTEM"]
        O1["CONTROLLER"] --> O2["PULSE GEN"]
        O2 --> O3["STEPPER DRIVE"]
        O3 --> O4["STEPPER MOTOR"]
        O4 --> O5["SCREW"]
        O5 --> O6["SLIDE"]
    end

    subgraph SEMI["SEMI CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        S1["CONTROLLER"] --> S2["COMPARATOR"]
        S2 --> S3["PID"]
        S3 --> S4["SERVO DRIVE"]
        S4 --> S5["SERVO MOTOR"]
        S5 --> S6["SCREW"]
        S6 --> S7["SLIDE"]
        S5 -->|"MOTOR ENCODER"| S2
    end

    subgraph CLOSED["FULLY CLOSED LOOP SYSTEM"]
        C1["CONTROLLER"] --> C2["COMPARATOR"]
        C2 --> C3["PID"]
        C3 --> C4["SERVO DRIVE"]
        C4 --> C5["SERVO MOTOR"]
        C5 --> C6["SCREW"]
        C6 --> C7["SLIDE"]
        C7 -->|"LINEAR SCALE"| C2
    end
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- **Open loop** has no return path.
- **Semi-closed loop** has the encoder on the **motor shaft** — feedback enters the comparator from the motor, not the slide.
- **Fully closed loop** has the linear scale on the **slide** — true mechanical position is fed back.

---

## 4.4 Error-Signal Flow Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["COMMAND Xc"] --> B["SUMMING JUNCTION SIGMA"]
    B --> C["ERROR e = Xc MINUS Xa"]
    C --> D["PID CONTROLLER"]
    D --> E["SERVO MOTOR"]
    E --> F["SLIDE POSITION Xa"]
    F -->|"FEEDBACK"| B
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the canonical control-theory block diagram. The loop closes through the feedback line returning to the summing junction. When $X_a$ matches $X_c$, the error $e$ becomes zero and the system reaches **steady state**.

---

## 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Choosing Open vs. Closed Loop in CNC Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: NEW CNC DESIGN"] --> B{"REQUIRED TOLERANCE?"}
    B -->|"ABOVE 0.05 MM"| C["OPEN LOOP STEPPER SYSTEM"]
    B -->|"0.005 TO 0.05 MM"| D["SEMI CLOSED LOOP SERVO"]
    B -->|"BELOW 0.005 MM"| E["FULLY CLOSED LOOP SERVO"]
    C --> F{"BUDGET CONSTRAINT?"}
    F -->|"TIGHT"| G["USE STEPPER MOTOR"]
    F -->|"MODERATE"| H["USE HYBRID STEPPER WITH ENCODER"]
    D --> I["USE SERVO MOTOR WITH ROTARY ENCODER"]
    E --> J["USE SERVO MOTOR WITH LINEAR GLASS SCALE"]
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the engineer's selection roadmap. Tolerance requirement is the primary design driver; cost is the secondary constraint.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
