# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between open-loop and closed-loop CNC control systems. State one typical application of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Feature | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Feedback** | Absent | Present (encoder/scale) |
| **Driving element** | Stepper motor | Servo motor |
| **Error correction** | Not possible | Continuous via PID |
| **Accuracy** | Moderate | High |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Application** | Desktop CNC router, 3D printer | Industrial CNC machining centre, EDM |

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- 1 Mark: Definition/feedback distinction
- 1 Mark: Driving element and accuracy
- 1 Mark: Application example

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**Q: What is a semi-closed loop CNC system? Why is it preferred over a fully closed-loop system in many industrial machines?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

A semi-closed loop system uses a **rotary encoder on the motor shaft** to provide feedback on rotor position, but does **not measure the actual slide position** directly. **(1 Mark)**

It is preferred because: **(2 Marks)**
1. It corrects motor-side errors (torque ripple, electrical errors) while avoiding the cost and fragility of mounting a linear scale on the slide.
2. It achieves accuracy adequate for most general machining (≈ 5 μm) at lower cost than a fully closed-loop system with a linear glass scale.
3. It avoids contamination-related failures of linear scales in harsh shop-floor environments.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

### Question A: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: Classify CNC control systems in detail. With the help of neat block diagrams, explain the working of open-loop and closed-loop CNC systems. Compare their performance in terms of accuracy, cost, complexity, and applications.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Classification and Open-Loop System

**Model Solution:**

**1. Classification of CNC Control Systems (3 Marks):**

CNC control systems are classified based on the **presence or absence of feedback**:

1. **Open-Loop System** — No feedback; uses stepper motors.
2. **Semi-Closed Loop System** — Feedback from motor-shaft encoder only.
3. **Closed-Loop System** — Feedback from slide-mounted linear scale; uses servo motors.

**2. Open-Loop System Working (4 Marks):**

The CNC controller generates a train of electrical pulses based on the part program. These pulses are sent to the stepper drive, which energizes the stepper motor windings in a precise sequence. Each pulse rotates the motor by a fixed step angle $\theta_s$ (commonly 1.8°). The motor is coupled to a lead/ball screw, which converts rotary motion into linear motion of the machine slide.

The linear displacement per pulse is:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{\theta_s \times L}{360° \times G}
$$

There is **no feedback element**; the system assumes the commanded motion has been executed. If the motor stalls due to overload, the slide position becomes erroneous silently.

**Block Diagram (text representation):**

```
Controller → Pulse Generator → Stepper Drive → Stepper Motor → Screw → Slide
```

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Closed-Loop System and Comparison

**Model Solution:**

**1. Closed-Loop System Working (4 Marks):**

The controller outputs a **commanded position** $X_c$. A position transducer (linear scale or encoder) measures the **actual position** $X_a$ and feeds it back to a comparator. The error signal is:

$$
e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)
$$

The PID controller processes this error and outputs a corrective voltage to the servo drive, which powers the servo motor. The motor drives the screw and slide. The loop continuously drives $e(t) \to 0$, ensuring the actual position converges to the commanded position.

**Block Diagram (text representation):**

```
Controller → Comparator ← Feedback (Linear Scale)
              ↓ (Error e)
            PID → Servo Drive → Servo Motor → Screw → Slide
                                                          ↓
                                                    Linear Scale ──┘
```

**2. Comparative Table (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Open-Loop | Closed-Loop |
|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | 0.01–0.05 mm | ≤ 0.001 mm |
| **Cost** | Low (no encoder) | High (encoder + drive) |
| **Complexity** | Simple | Complex (tuning needed) |
| **Applications** | Hobby CNC, 3D printers | Industrial machining, EDM |
| **Stall Detection** | None | Yes (error alarm) |

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Part (a): 3 marks classification + 4 marks open-loop working
- Part (b): 4 marks closed-loop working + 3 marks comparison

---

### Question B (Internal Choice Alternative): [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: With neat block diagrams, explain the working of (i) open-loop and (ii) closed-loop CNC systems. Derive the expression for the linear displacement per pulse in an open-loop system, and the steady-state error of a closed-loop system with proportional control.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Block Diagrams and Open-Loop Derivation

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagrams (4 Marks):**

**Open-Loop:**
```
CNC Controller → Pulse Generator → Stepper Drive → Stepper Motor → Lead Screw → Slide
```

**Closed-Loop:**
```
CNC Controller → Comparator ← Linear Scale (on slide)
                       ↓
                    PID → Servo Drive → Servo Motor → Lead Screw → Slide
                                                                       ↑
                                                              Linear Scale
```

**Derivation — Linear Displacement per Pulse (3 Marks):**

Given:
- Step angle $\theta_s$
- Screw lead $L$ (mm/rev)
- Gear ratio $G$

Per pulse, the motor rotates by $\theta_s$. The fraction of one full revolution per pulse is:

$$
f_{\text{rev}} = \frac{\theta_s}{360°}
$$

Linear displacement per pulse is:

$$
\Delta x = f_{\text{rev}} \times \frac{L}{G} = \frac{\theta_s \times L}{360° \times G}
$$

For $\theta_s = 1.8°$, $L = 10$ mm, $G = 1$:

$$
\Delta x = \frac{1.8 \times 10}{360} = 0.05 \text{ mm/pulse} = 50 \text{ μm/pulse}
$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Closed-Loop Steady-State Error Derivation

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Command signal $X_c(t)$
- Actual position $X_a(t)$
- Proportional gain $K_p$
- Motor gain $K_m$

**Step 1 — Error signal:**

$$
e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)
$$

**Step 2 — Control law (P-only):**

$$
u(t) = K_p \, e(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Motor-load first-order dynamics:**

$$
\tau \frac{dX_a}{dt} + X_a = K_m \, u(t)
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute control law:**

$$
\tau \frac{dX_a}{dt} + X_a = K_m K_p (X_c - X_a)
$$

$$
\tau \frac{dX_a}{dt} + (1 + K_m K_p) X_a = K_m K_p \, X_c
$$

**Step 5 — Steady state** ($\dfrac{dX_a}{dt} = 0$):

$$
X_a^{ss} = \frac{K_m K_p}{1 + K_m K_p} X_c^{ss}
$$

**Step 6 — Steady-state error:**

$$
\boxed{\; e^{ss} = X_c^{ss} - X_a^{ss} = \frac{X_c^{ss}}{1 + K_m K_p} = \frac{X_c^{ss}}{1 + K_L}\;}
$$

where $K_L = K_m K_p$ is the loop gain.

**Interpretation (1 Mark):** As $K_L \to \infty$, the steady-state error approaches zero — explaining the high accuracy of closed-loop CNC systems.

**Mark Distribution (KTU pattern):**
- Part (a): 4 marks diagrams + 3 marks derivation
- Part (b): 6 marks derivation + 1 mark interpretation

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Confusing semi-closed with fully closed loop:** Many students write "semi-closed loop is the same as closed loop." **Penalty: −2 marks.** Clearly state the feedback is on the **motor shaft**, not the slide.
> 2. **Omitting units in numerical answers:** Writing "0.05" without "mm/pulse" loses 0.5–1 mark.
> 3. **Writing "open-loop is inaccurate":** Use precise language — "open-loop is *less accurate* under dynamic loading" or "limited by step resolution."
> 4. **Forgetting the feedback line in block diagrams:** The arrow from the position sensor back to the comparator is mandatory. Drawing an open-loop diagram for a closed-loop question = −3 marks.
> 5. **Skipping the "engineering interpretation" line:** KTU 2024 scheme rewards applied thinking. Always end a derivation with one sentence on what the result means in practice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read this the night before the KTU exam.**

### 🔑 Key Definitions
- **Open-loop CNC:** No feedback. Uses **stepper motor**. Each pulse = fixed step.
- **Closed-loop CNC:** Has feedback. Uses **servo motor + encoder/scale + PID**.
- **Semi-closed loop:** Feedback on **motor shaft only**; not on slide.

### 🔑 Must-Remember Formulas
- Linear displacement per pulse: $\Delta x = \dfrac{\theta_s \cdot L}{360° \cdot G}$
- Closed-loop error: $e(t) = X_c(t) - X_a(t)$
- Steady-state error (P-control): $e^{ss} = \dfrac{X_c^{ss}}{1 + K_L}$
- Required pulse frequency: $f = \dfrac{v_{\text{feed}} \cdot 360° \cdot G}{\theta_s \cdot L}$

### 🔑 Key Numbers to Memorize
- Common step angle: **1.8°** (200 steps/rev)
- Common step angle: **0.72°** (500 steps/rev — higher resolution)
- Accuracy benchmark: open-loop ≈ **0.01–0.05 mm**; closed-loop ≈ **≤ 0.001 mm**

### 🔑 Advantages vs. Disadvantages (One-Line Memory)
- **Open = cheap & simple but blind** (no stall detection)
- **Closed = expensive & accurate but tuned** (PID tuning required)
- **Semi-closed = compromise** (industry favourite)

### 🔑 Application Match
- Hobby CNC / 3D printer → **Open loop (stepper)**
- Industrial CNC machining centre → **Semi-closed loop (servo + rotary encoder)**
- Precision EDM / aerospace CNC → **Fully closed loop (servo + linear glass scale)**

### 🔑 Exam-Writing Strategy (KTU 2024)
1. Always **draw the block diagram first** — it earns easy 2–3 marks.
2. Use **tables for comparison** — examiners scan for structured answers.
3. End numerical answers with an **interpretation sentence** — earns the "applied thinking" mark.
4. **Tag your answer to the system type** (open/semi-closed/closed) in the first line.
5. For 14-mark questions, allocate roughly **7 marks per sub-part**.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Notes — KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 | PEMET866 Module 1**
> *All formulas, derivations, and question patterns are aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Revised Bloom's Taxonomy (Levels 2–4: Understand, Apply, Analyze).*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of CNC Machine Tools and System Devices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET866 - ADVANCED NUMERICAL CONTROL IN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Types of CNC Machine Tools and System Devices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are CNC Machine Tools and System Devices?

A **CNC (Computer Numerical Control) Machine Tool** is an advanced, automated manufacturing system in which the entire machining process — including motion of slides, spindle speed, feed rate, tool changes, and coolant flow — is precisely controlled by a dedicated **Machine Control Unit (MCU)** executing a stored part program. The MCU receives coded instructions (typically in **G-code and M-code**, conforming to **ISO 6983 / EIA RS-267-D** standards) and translates them into coordinated electrical signals that drive the machine axes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> A CNC machine tool is a reprogrammable, multi-functional machine whose control functions, including axis interpolation, spindle synchronization, and tool management, are performed by a computer-based controller using stored numerical data derived from coded alphanumeric instructions.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Self-Driving Car" of Manufacturing

Think of a CNC machine like a **self-driving car**:

| Self-Driving Car (Analogy) | CNC Machine (Reality) |
|---|---|
| GPS + Map (destination input) | Part Program (G-code / M-code) |
| Onboard Computer (decision-making) | Machine Control Unit (MCU / CNC Controller) |
| Electric Motors (wheels turning) | Servo/Stepper Drives + Lead Screws/Linear Guides |
| Speed & Steering Sensors | Encoders, Resolvers, Glass Scales (Feedback) |
| Steering, Brake, Accelerator | Slideways, Spindle, Tool Magazine (Actuators) |
| Dashboard Display | HMI / CRT/LCD Operator Panel |

Just as the car *interprets* digital maps and *actuates* physical motion, the CNC machine *interprets* alphanumeric codes and *actuates* cutting motion. The "feedback sensors" (encoders) constantly check *where the slide actually is* versus *where it should be*, correcting errors in real time — this is the heart of **closed-loop control**.

## 1.2 Classification of CNC Machine Tools — Two Broad Lenses

CNC machines are classified in **two principal ways** in the KTU syllabus:

### A. By **Motion-Control / Path Type** (Kinematic Classification)

1. **Point-to-Point (PTP) / Positioning Systems** — e.g., CNC Drilling, Punching, Boring machines. Tool moves to a coordinate, then cuts. No controlled motion during positioning.
2. **Straight-Cut / Line-Cut (1D Contouring)** — Tool moves in straight lines between two points; only one axis moves at a time. e.g., simple milling.
3. **Continuous Path / Contouring (2D & 2½D)** — Two or more axes move **simultaneously and in coordination** to generate curves. e.g., CNC lathe (2-axis), vertical machining center.
4. **Multi-Axis Contouring (3D, 4-axis, 5-axis)** — Three or more axes interpolated simultaneously for complex 3D surfaces, free-form dies, blisks, etc.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Frequently Asked Distinction:**
> - *Point-to-Point* machines use **rapid traverse (G00)** for positioning, cutting is done only *after* arrival.
> - *Contouring* machines use **linear interpolation (G01)** or **circular interpolation (G02/G03)** during cutting — *both axes are driven simultaneously* to maintain a tool path geometry.

### B. By **Process / Configuration Type** (Functional Classification)

| Category | Typical CNC Variants | Primary Operation |
|---|---|---|
| **Turning** | CNC Lathe, Slant-bed Lathe, Turning Center, Multi-axis Turning Center, Swiss-type Lathe | Cylindrical turning, facing, threading, grooving |
| **Milling** | Vertical Machining Center (VMC), Horizontal Machining Center (HMC), Bed Mill, 5-Axis Machining Center | Slot, pocket, profile, 3D surface milling |
| **Drilling & Tapping** | CNC Drilling Machine, Tapping Center, Multi-spindle Drilling | Hole making, tapping, reaming |
| **Grinding** | CNC Cylindrical Grinder, Surface Grinder, Tool & Cutter Grinder, Jig Grinder | Precision finishing of hardened surfaces |
| **EDM (Electrical Discharge)** | Die-sink EDM (Ram/Sinker), Wire-Cut EDM, Hole-Drilling EDM | Hard-material shaping, intricate cavities |
| **Hybrid / Special** | Water-Jet, Laser, Plasma CNC cutters, Additive+Subtractive Hybrid | Cutting, additive manufacturing |
| **Multi-Tasking** | Mill-Turn, Turn-Mill, Boring-Mill | Combined turning + milling in one setup |

> [!NOTE]
> **Modern Terminology — Why "Center" matters:**
> A **Machining Center** (MC) = CNC mill **plus** Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) **plus** Pallet Changer **plus** Coolant & Chip Management. A plain CNC milling machine lacks the ATC and is therefore *not* a true machining center.

## 1.3 System Devices — The Six Functional Subsystems

A CNC machine is an *integration* of six major sub-systems:

1. **Input / Program Storage Media** — Punch tape (legacy), magnetic tape, floppy, USB, Ethernet, DNC (Direct Numerical Control) link.
2. **Machine Control Unit (MCU / Controller)** — CNC computer (e.g., FANUC, Siemens SINUMERIK, Mitsubishi M80, Heidenhain TNC).
3. **Drive System** — Servo motors (AC brushless, DC), stepper motors, hydraulic/pneumatic actuators.
4. **Feedback / Measurement System** — Rotary encoders, linear encoders (glass/steel scales), resolvers, LVDTs, Inductosyn.
5. **Machine Tool Structure & Slide Elements** — Cast iron/Polymer concrete bed, linear roller/ball guides, ball screws/rack-and-pinion.
6. **Auxiliary Devices** — Automatic Tool Changer (ATC), Tool Magazine, Pallet Changer, Coolant system, Chip conveyor, Lubrication, Work-holding fixtures, Safety interlocks, HMI panel.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 3-Loop Control Architecture (a KTU favorite):**
> - **Position Loop** (outermost) — Compares commanded vs. actual position (uses feedback scale).
> - **Velocity Loop** (middle) — Compares commanded vs. actual velocity (uses tachometer or encoder-derived velocity).
> - **Current Loop** (innermost) — Compares commanded vs. actual motor current (torque control).

---

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification by Motion Type — Detailed Logic

### 2.1.1 Point-to-Point (PTP) Systems

- **Logic:** Each axis is driven **independently** to a programmed coordinate at **rapid traverse (G00)**.
- **Tool path accuracy is required only at the *end* of the move**, not during transit.
- **Typical use:** Drilling, tapping, reaming, spot facing, boring.
- **Drive complexity:** Lowest. Stepper motors or open-loop servo systems are often sufficient for short-travel, low-force PTP.
- **Limitation:** Cannot produce accurate contours because path *between* points is uncontrolled.

### 2.1.2 Straight-Line (Line-Cut) Systems

- **Logic:** Only **one axis moves at a time** during cutting, but at *controlled feed rate (G01)*.
- **Result:** A 2D shape is built from a *sequence of axis-aligned straight segments* — like raster-scanning a line drawing.
- **Use case:** Simple rectangular pockets, stepped slots, straight-line engraving.
- **Limitation:** Diagonal edges require many short steps → poor finish & long cycle time.

### 2.1.3 Contouring (Continuous Path) Systems

- **Logic:** **Two or more axes are driven simultaneously and their instantaneous velocities are coordinated** by the controller's **interpolator** (linear G01, circular G02/G03, helical, parabolic, cubic spline).
- **Result:** Smooth curves, circles, tapers, and complex 2D/3D profiles in a single uninterrupted move.
- **Drive complexity:** Highest. Requires **closed-loop servo** on every controlled axis, with a real-time interpolator.
- **Use case:** Almost all modern CNC lathes, mills, EDM, and grinders.

### 2.1.4 Multi-Axis Contouring (4-/5-Axis)

- **4-axis:** Adds one rotational axis (A or B) — used for wrap-around milling, propellers, turbine blades.
- **5-axis:** Adds two rotational axes (typically A + C or B + C) — used for aerospace blisks, medical implants, dies & molds.
- **Interpolation** now spans **3 linear + 2 rotational** = **5D vector** coordinated in real time at the **block processing time (BT)** of the controller (often **1 ms or less**).

> [!TIP]
> **Why does axis count matter for KTU problems?**
> The number of *interpolated axes* (i.e., axes moving simultaneously under coordinated control) defines the machine's true *contouring capability*. A "5-axis machine" only delivers full 5-axis contouring if its controller supports **5-axis simultaneous interpolation** — many "5-axis machines" sold in industry are actually 3+2 (positional 5-axis, not simultaneous).

## 2.2 System Device Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 Input Devices & Program Transfer

| Era | Media | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| 1950s–1970s | **1-inch punched paper tape** (EIA RS-244 / ASCII) | Fragile, low density (~8 chars/inch), noisy readers |
| 1970s–1990s | Magnetic tape, 8-inch floppy, RS-232 serial | Faster, higher density |
| 1990s–2010s | 3.5" floppy, RS-232, RS-422, Ethernet (TCP/IP) | Network-capable |
| 2010s–present | **USB stick, Ethernet, Wi-Fi, OPC-UA, MT-Connect** | Industry 4.0, real-time DNC |

- **DNC (Direct Numerical Control):** A host computer directly feeds blocks of NC code to one or many CNC machines *in real time* over a network. Modern DNC supports **DNC-FTP**, **FTP over Ethernet**, and even **wireless DNC**.
- **MDI (Manual Data Input):** Operator types blocks at the controller panel for one-off operations.
- **Conversational Programming:** Operator-friendly graphical input (e.g., Mazatrol, Heidenhain Klartext) — no G-code needed.

### 2.2.2 Machine Control Unit (MCU)

The MCU is the **brain**. It contains:

- **CPU** (32-bit / 64-bit microprocessor) executing the part program.
- **Memory hierarchy:** ROM (firmware), RAM (program + buffers), CMOS (parameters), Flash (NC data).
- **Interpolator** (linear, circular, helical, spline).
- **PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) — soft-PLC inside MCU** handling auxiliary functions: coolant on/off, tool change, door interlock, spindle start/stop.
- **HMI (Human-Machine Interface):** Color LCD/CRT, push-buttons, MPG (Manual Pulse Generator) / handwheel.
- **I/O interfaces:** RS-232C, RS-422, USB, Ethernet, Profibus/Profinet, EtherCAT.

**Block diagram of MCU signal flow:**

```
Part Program → Reader/DNC → CPU → Interpolator → Reference Pulses (θ_ref)
                                                            ↓
                                                    Servo Amplifier (Drive)
                                                            ↓
                                                          Servo Motor
                                                            ↓
                                                         Ball Screw
                                                            ↓
                                                         Machine Slide
                                                            ↓
                                                    Encoder (θ_actual) →
                                                            ↑ feedback
```

### 2.2.3 Drive Systems

| Drive Type | Principle | Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Stepper Motor (open-loop)** | Discrete angular steps; no feedback needed | Small PTP machines, low-cost routers, 3D printers |
| **DC Servo Motor** | DC brush + tachogenerator feedback | Older CNC (1970s–80s) |
| **AC Brushless Servo Motor** | Permanent-magnet rotor + 3-phase inverter (PWM) | Modern CNC — high speed, high torque, low heat |
| **Linear Motor** | Direct linear thrust, no ball screw | Ultra-high-speed milling, laser cutters |
| **Hydraulic Servo** | Hydraulic cylinder + servo valve | Heavy-duty machines, large lathes, presses |
| **Pneumatic** | Air cylinder | Indexing tables, clamps, tool release |

> [!NOTE]
> **Open-loop vs. Closed-loop** (high-yield KTU point):
> - **Open-loop (stepper):** MCU sends N pulses → motor steps N × (step angle). If load stalls, *no correction* — silent failure.
> - **Closed-loop (servo):** Encoder continuously reports actual position; MCU's **position-error counter** computes the *lag* (following error) and adjusts drive voltage/frequency every cycle (typically every **1–4 ms**).

### 2.2.4 Feedback Devices

| Sensor | Type | Function | Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Rotary Encoder (Incremental)** | Optical / Magnetic | Position of motor shaft (after gearing) | 2,500 – 100,000 ppr |
| **Rotary Encoder (Absolute)** | Optical | True absolute position; no re-homing after power off | Up to 25 bits |
| **Linear Encoder / Glass Scale** | Optical | Direct position of slide (full-closed loop) | 0.1 µm – 1 µm |
| **Resolver** | Electromagnetic | Rugged position sensor (servo) | 4,096 – 16,384 counts |
| **LVDT** | Electromagnetic | Linear displacement (mostly hydraulic servos) | Sub-µm |
| **Inductosyn** | Electromagnetic | Very long linear scale (machine tools) | 0.5 µm |
| **Tachogenerator** | Electromagnetic | Velocity feedback (legacy) | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Semi-closed vs. Full-closed Loop — the KTU classic:**
> - **Semi-closed loop:** Encoder on motor → measures shaft angle → MCU infers slide position by multiplying by gear/screw ratio. Ball-screw pitch error *is not* corrected.
> - **Full-closed loop:** Linear scale mounted directly on the slide → measures true slide position → corrects *all* errors (thermal expansion, screw wear, backlash). Mandatory for ultra-precision (µm-level) machines.

### 2.2.5 Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) & Tool Magazine

- **Tool Magazine Types:** Drum, disk (carousel), chain, matrix, rack.
- **Typical magazine capacity:** 12 → 30 → 60 → 120 → 240+ tools.
- **Tool-change time:** Modern VMCs: 0.5 – 1.5 s (chip-to-chip).
- **Tool identification:** Barcode, RFID, mechanical slot, optical.
- **Tool-length & radius offset:** Stored in controller's offset table; called up via **G43 H##** and **D##**.

### 2.2.6 Work-Holding Devices

- Standard chucks (3-jaw, 4-jaw, collet), face plates, steady/follow rests (lathes).
- Vises, clamps, vacuum tables, magnetic chucks (mills/grinders).
- **Pallet Changer:** Swaps entire workpiece on a pallet → operator loads next pallet during machining → near-zero idle time (popular in FMS).

### 2.2.7 Coolant, Lubrication & Chip Management

- Flood coolant, through-spindle coolant (high-pressure, 70+ bar), MQL (Minimum Quantity Lubrication).
- Automatic lubrication for ball screws and guides.
- Chip conveyor (hinged belt, scraper, auger, magnetic).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit / Typical Value | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Following error (E) | $E = X_{\text{commanded}} - X_{\text{actual}}$ | µm, mm | Lag between commanded and actual position |
| 2 | Position-loop gain (K_p) | $K_p = \dfrac{V_{\text{feed}}}{E_{\text{ss}}}$ | 1/s or mm/min/mm | Stiffness of servo response |
| 3 | Feed rate from spindle speed & feed/rev | $F = N \times f_r$ | mm/min | $N$ = rpm, $f_r$ = mm/rev |
| 4 | Feed rate from tooth engagement | $F = N \times z \times f_z$ | mm/min | $z$ = number of teeth, $f_z$ = feed/tooth |
| 5 | MRR (Material Removal Rate) milling | $\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F$ | mm³/min | $a_p$ = depth, $a_e$ = width, $F$ = feed |
| 6 | MRR turning | $\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f_r \cdot N$ | mm³/min | $D$ = dia, $L$ = length |
| 7 | Cutting speed turning | $V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N$ | m/min | Linear surface speed |
| 8 | Spindle rpm from cutting speed | $N = \dfrac{V_c \cdot 1000}{\pi \cdot D}$ | rpm | — |
| 9 | Block Processing Time (BPT) | $\text{BPT} = \dfrac{L_{\text{block}}}{F} \cdot 60$ | ms | Time to execute one NC block |
| 10 | Tool diameter compensation path offset | $R_{\text{offset}} = R_{\text{tool}} \pm \delta_{\text{cutter}}$ | mm | Cutter comp sign by G41/G42 |
| 11 | Ball-screw lead accuracy | $E_{\text{pitch}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{actual}} - P_{\text{nominal}}}{P_{\text{nominal}}} \cdot 10^6$ | µm / 300 mm | ISO 3408 grade C0, C1, C3, C5, C7 |
| 12 | Servo motor torque demand | $T = F \cdot \dfrac{L}{2\pi \cdot \eta} \cdot \dfrac{1}{i}$ | N·m | $F$ = thrust, $L$ = screw lead, $\eta$ = eff., $i$ = ratio |
| 13 | Following error limit (approx) | $E_{\text{max}} \leq \dfrac{F}{60 \cdot K_v}$ | mm | $K_v$ = position-loop gain (1/s) |
| 14 | Dwell time | $G04 \, P\_$ (seconds) | s | Pause for hole-bottom or tool change |
| 15 | Polar-coordinate interpolation radius | $R = \sqrt{X^2 + Y^2}$ | mm | Required for 2-axis contouring |

> [!NOTE]
> For exam use, **memorize formulas (3), (5), (7), (8), (12)** — these are the most-asked in KTU Module-1 numericals.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace** — 5-axis contouring of blisks & turbine blades (e.g., GE LEAP engine blades, single-setup accuracy ± 0.02 mm).
- **Automotive** — High-volume engine block, transmission housing, brake-disc machining on FMS with DNC + pallet changers.
- **Medical** — Custom orthopedic implants, dental crowns (5-axis wet/Dry mill).
- **Die & Mold** — Graphite/copper electrode milling, injection-mold cavities with µm-level contouring.
- **Electronics** — PCB drilling/routing (multi-spindle PTP) and wafer dicing.
- **Industry 4.0** — All modern devices integrate with MT-Connect, OPC-UA, predictive-maintenance dashboards, and digital twins.

---

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Optimum Spindle Speed for a Given Cutting Speed

**Problem statement:** A CNC turning center must machine an EN8 steel shaft of diameter $D = 60$ mm at a recommended cutting speed $V_c = 180$ m/min. Determine the spindle rpm $N$ and the corresponding feed rate $F$ if a feed-per-revolution $f_r = 0.25$ mm/rev is specified.

**Step 1 — State the fundamental kinematic relation.**

For a turning operation, the linear surface speed at the cutting edge is related to the spindle rotational speed and the workpiece diameter by:

$$
V_c = \pi \cdot D \cdot N
$$

where $V_c$ is in mm/min when $D$ is in mm and $N$ is in rpm. (To get m/min, divide by 1 000.)

**Step 2 — Express $N$ explicitly.**

$$
N = \dfrac{V_c}{\pi \cdot D} \quad \text{(with consistent units)}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert $V_c$ from m/min to mm/min.**

$$
V_c = 180 \ \text{m/min} = 180 \times 1000 = 180\,000 \ \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$
N = \dfrac{180\,000}{\pi \times 60}
$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate.**

$$
N = \dfrac{180\,000}{188.4956} = 954.93 \ \text{rpm}
$$

**Step 6 — Round to a programmable, controller-friendly value.**

Typically, the controller accepts integer rpm. Round to the nearest standard step (often 1 rpm on modern CNC, 10 rpm on older):

$$
\boxed{N \approx 955 \ \text{rpm}}
$$

**Step 7 — Compute the feed rate using $F = N \times f_r$.**

$$
F = 954.93 \times 0.25
$$

**Step 8 — Evaluate.**

$$
F = 238.73 \ \text{mm/min}
$$

**Step 9 — Round.**

$$
\boxed{F \approx 239 \ \text{mm/min}}
$$

> **Result Summary:**
> - $N = 955$ rpm
> - $F = 239$ mm/min
> - **NC block (Fanuc-style):** `G97 S955 M03; G00 X62.0 Z2.0; G01 Z-50.0 F239;`

## 3.2 Derivation: Position-Loop Gain and Following-Error Trade-off

**Problem statement:** A CNC milling machine is required to contour a pocket at $F = 2\,000$ mm/min while keeping the steady-state following error below $E_{\max} = 0.05$ mm. Determine the **minimum position-loop gain** $K_v$ required (in 1/s).

**Step 1 — Define following error in terms of feed and gain.**

The classical first-order approximation of a CNC servo gives the steady-state following error as:

$$
E_{ss} = \dfrac{F}{60 \cdot K_v}
$$

where $F$ is in mm/min and $K_v$ is in 1/s, so that $F/60$ converts to mm/s.

**Step 2 — Rearrange for $K_v$.**

$$
K_v = \dfrac{F}{60 \cdot E_{ss}}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $F = 2000$ mm/min, $E_{ss} = 0.05$ mm.**

$$
K_v = \dfrac{2000}{60 \times 0.05}
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate.**

$$
K_v = \dfrac{2000}{3} = 666.67 \ \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5 — Round up for safety margin (typically +10 %).**

$$
\boxed{K_v \geq 666.7 \ \text{s}^{-1}}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A high $K_v$ means a *stiff* servo — small following error and fast response, but more prone to overshoot, chatter, and acoustic noise. Industrial CNC controllers expose $K_v$ as a tuning parameter (loop gain) in the *servo parameter sheet* (e.g., FANUC parameter 1820, Siemens MD32250).

## 3.3 Derivation: Semi-Closed vs Full-Closed Loop — Resolution Comparison

**Problem statement:** A machining center uses a rotary encoder on the servo motor (semi-closed loop) and a ball screw with lead $L = 10$ mm/rev. The encoder has $C = 20\,000$ pulses per revolution, multiplied by the controller's electronic gearing $E_g = 4$ (quadrature). The ball-screw pitch error is $E_p = 8$ µm per 300 mm.

(a) Compute the **slide resolution** (smallest commanded increment).
(b) Compute the **worst-case positioning error** of the slide.
(c) Comment on whether a **linear scale (full-closed loop)** is justified.

**Step 1 — Slide resolution (semi-closed).**

$$
\text{Res}_{\text{slide}} = \dfrac{L}{C \times E_g} = \dfrac{10 \ \text{mm}}{20\,000 \times 4}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Res}_{\text{slide}} = 0.000125 \ \text{mm} = 0.125 \ \mu\text{m}}
$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case position error over 300 mm of travel.**

$$
E_{\text{worst}} = E_p + E_{\text{enc}} \approx 8 \ \mu\text{m} + 0.125 \ \mu\text{m} \approx 8.125 \ \mu\text{m}
$$

(Encoder contribution is negligible; ball-screw pitch error dominates.)

**Step 3 — With a linear scale of resolution 0.1 µm, the position error reduces to:**

$$
E_{\text{full}} \leq 0.1 \ \mu\text{m} + \text{linear-scale non-linearity} \approx 1 \ \mu\text{m (typically)}
$$

**Step 4 — Justification.** A linear scale is justified when:
- Required positioning tolerance is $< 5$ µm.
- Ball-screw wear/thermal growth is non-negligible.
- Machine is used for precision die/mold work or optical/lens machining.

> **Conclusion:** For an ultra-precision machine, the **linear scale (full-closed loop)** is mandatory; for a general-purpose VMC, a semi-closed loop is acceptable.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — CNC System Devices & Their Selection

| Subsystem | Selection Criteria | Common Choice (Small Workshop) | Common Choice (Industry) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Input | Program size, connectivity | USB stick | Ethernet DNC, MT-Connect |
| MCU | Complexity of parts, axes | 3-axis Fanuc 0i-MF | 5-axis Siemens SINUMERIK ONE |
| Servo drive | Torque, speed, accuracy | Stepper (open-loop) | AC brushless servo + linear scale |
| Feedback | Tolerance class | Incremental rotary encoder | Absolute linear glass scale |
| Tool magazine | Part family variety | 12-station drum | 60+ tool chain magazine |
| Work-holding | Batch vs. job shop | 3-jaw manual chuck | 4th-axis trunnion + pallet changer |
| Coolant | Material & tool life | Flood coolant | High-pressure through-spindle + MQL |
| Controller platform | Operator skill, language | Conversational (Mazatrol) | Full G-code (Fanuc / Siemens) |

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Six Subsystems of a CNC Machine

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Part Program Storage Media] --> B[Machine Control Unit MCU]
    B --> C[Interpolator and PLC]
    C --> D[Servo Amplifiers]
    D --> E[Servo Motors]
    E --> F[Ball Screw and Linear Guide]
    F --> G[Machine Slide and Spindle]
    H[Feedback Encoders] --> B
    I[Linear Scale Glass Scale] --> B
    J[Automatic Tool Changer ATC] --> G
    K[Tool Magazine] --> J
    L[Coolant and Lubrication] --> G
    M[Work Holding Chuck Vise] --> G
    N[HMI Operator Panel MPG Handwheel] --> B
    O[Host Computer DNC Network] --> A
```

> **Reading the diagram:** A and O feed data to the brain B (MCU). B uses H and I to know the *true* position. C (interpolator + PLC) breaks the program into axis commands, which D (servo amp) amplifies into E (motor) motion. E drives F (mechanical transmission) which moves G (slide/spindle/tool). J, K, L, M are auxiliary devices managed by the PLC inside B. N is the operator's window into the system.

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Three-Loop Servo Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    ref[Reference Pulses from Interpolator] --> posSum((Summing Junction Position Loop))
    fbPos[Linear Scale Feedback] --> posSum
    posSum --> posErr[Position Error e_p]
    posErr --> velLoop[Velocity Loop Controller]
    fbVel[Tachometer or Encoder Velocity] --> velLoop
    velLoop --> curLoop[Current Loop Controller]
    fbCur[Current Sensor] --> curLoop
    curLoop --> motor[Servo Motor PWM Drive]
    motor --> screw[Ball Screw and Slide]
    screw --> fbPos
    screw --> fbVel
    motor --> fbCur
```

> **Note on the three loops:** Outermost is the **position loop** (slowest, ~1 ms), middle is the **velocity loop** (~250 µs), and innermost is the **current/torque loop** (~62.5 µs). The hierarchy ensures that *torque* is controlled fastest, *velocity* second, and *position* last — this guarantees mechanical stability.

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Classification Tree of CNC Machines

```mermaid
graph TD
    root[CNC Machine Tools]
    root --> mc[Motion Control Type]
    root --> pc[Process Configuration Type]
    mc --> m1[Point to Point PTP]
    mc --> m2[Straight Cut 1D]
    mc --> m3[Contouring 2D 2.5D]
    mc --> m4[Multi Axis Contouring 3D 4 Axis 5 Axis]
    m1 --> ex1[Drilling Tapping Boring Punching]
    m2 --> ex2[Simple Slotting Engraving]
    m3 --> ex3[CNC Lathe 2 Axis VMC]
    m4 --> ex4[Aerospace Blade Mold Implant]
    pc --> p1[Turning Machines]
    pc --> p2[Milling Machines]
    pc --> p3[Drilling and Tapping Centers]
    pc --> p4[Grinding Machines]
    pc --> p5[EDM Die Sinker and Wire Cut]
    pc --> p6[Hybrid Laser Water Jet]
    pc --> p7[Multi Tasking Mill Turn]
    p1 --> t1[CNC Lathe Turning Center Slant Bed]
    p2 --> t2[VMC HMC 5 Axis Machining Center]
    p5 --> e1[Die Sink Ram EDM Wire Cut EDM]
    p7 --> mt1[Mill Turn Turn Mill Boring Mill]
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Feedback Device Selection Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Need Position Feedback] --> q1{Tolerance Required}
    q1 -->|less than 5 micrometer| fl[Full Closed Linear Scale Glass Scale]
    q1 -->|5 to 50 micrometer| sm[Semi Closed Rotary Encoder on Motor]
    q1 -->|more than 50 micrometer low cost| op[Open Loop Stepper Motor]
    fl --> a1[Use Invar or Steel Scale]
    sm --> a2[Use Incremental 20k ppr Encoder]
    op --> a3[No Feedback Required]
    fl --> env{Environment}
    env -->|Clean Lab| glass[Glass Scale 0.1 micrometer]
    env -->|Workshop| steel[Steel Tape Scale 1 micrometer]
    sm --> res{Resolution Needed}
    res -->|High| abso[Absolute Encoder 25 bit]
    res -->|Medium| incr[Incremental Encoder 2500 ppr]
```

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, 3 marks]**
> **Differentiate between Point-to-Point (PTP) and Continuous Path (Contouring) CNC machines. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Point-to-Point (PTP) | Continuous Path (Contouring) |
|---|---|---|
| Axis motion | Each axis moves **independently** at rapid traverse | **Two or more axes move simultaneously**, coordinated by the interpolator |
| Tool path accuracy | Required **only at the end-point** of motion | Required **throughout the entire move** |
| Interpolator | Not required | Required (linear, circular, helical) |
| Typical commands | G00, G81-G89 (drilling cycles) | G01, G02, G03, G06.2 spline |
| Examples | CNC drilling machine, CNC punching press, CNC boring | CNC lathe, VMC, 5-axis machining center |
| Cost / complexity | Lower | Higher (closed-loop servos on all axes) |

**Example of PTP:** CNC drilling machine (G81 drill cycle — drill moves in Z only at rapid to start position).
**Example of Contouring:** CNC turning center generating a 30° taper with G01 simultaneous X & Z move.

> **Examiner Note (3 marks breakdown):**
> - (1 mark) — Clear definition of each type.
> - (1 mark) — Tabulated difference (any 4 of the 6 above).
> - (1 mark) — One correct example each.

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, 3 marks]**
> **List the major sub-systems of a CNC machine tool and briefly explain the function of the Machine Control Unit (MCU).**

**Model Answer:**

The **six major sub-systems** of a CNC machine tool are:
1. Program input / storage media
2. Machine Control Unit (MCU)
3. Drive system (servo/stepper)
4. Feedback / measurement system
5. Machine tool structure (slides, spindle, ATC)
6. Auxiliary devices (coolant, lubrication, work-holding, HMI)

**Function of the MCU** (≈ 1.5 marks):
The MCU is the **brain** of the CNC machine. It:
- **Reads and decodes** the part program (G-code, M-code) from the input media.
- Performs **interpolation** (linear, circular, helical) to generate smooth coordinated axis motion.
- **Regulates servo drives** by closing the position, velocity, and current loops.
- Runs an **integrated soft-PLC** that handles auxiliary functions: tool change, coolant on/off, spindle start, door interlock, lubrication.
- Communicates with the **HMI** for operator input, alarms, and diagnostics.
- Stores **tool offsets, work offsets, parameters, and ladder logic** in non-volatile memory.

> **Examiner Note:** Full marks only if the student names ≥ 4 sub-systems (1.5 marks) and gives ≥ 3 distinct MCU functions (1.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 PART B — Essay / Numerical Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Instruction (as per KTU 2024 ESE):**
> Answer **ONE** of the following: **Question A or Question B.**

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1]**

**(a)** Classify CNC machine tools based on **(i)** motion control path and **(ii)** process configuration. With neat sketches, explain **Point-to-Point, Straight-cut, and Contouring** motion types. Give one example machine for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **function of feedback devices** in a CNC servo loop. With a neat block diagram, explain the **three-loop control architecture** (position, velocity, current). Compare **semi-closed and full-closed loop** configurations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Motion Types — 7 marks:**

*Classification table:*

| Basis | Categories |
|---|---|
| Motion control path | PTP, Straight-cut, Contouring (2D / 2.5D / 3D / 4-axis / 5-axis) |
| Process configuration | Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, EDM, Laser/Water-jet, Multi-tasking |

*Three motion types — explanation:*

1. **Point-to-Point (PTP):** Tool moves at rapid traverse to the target coordinate; cutting happens *only* at the destination. Axis motions are *sequential*, not *simultaneous*. **Example:** CNC drilling machine using G81 cycle — the drill moves from current X,Y to X_new, Y_new at G00, *then* plunges in Z. (2 marks)

2. **Straight-Cut (1D):** Only **one axis moves at a time** during cutting at *controlled* feed (G01). 2D shapes are built by stitching axis-aligned line segments. **Example:** Simple engraving of straight text using G01 with single-axis moves. (2 marks)

3. **Contouring (Continuous Path):** **Two or more axes move simultaneously**, with velocities controlled by the **interpolator** so that the tool follows a prescribed geometric path (straight line, arc, spline, 3D surface). **Example:** CNC turning a taper (X and Z simultaneous), or a VMC milling a circular pocket (G02/G03). (2 marks)

*Diagrams:* (Three simple block diagrams showing axis arrows — single for PTP, sequential for straight-cut, simultaneous for contouring.) (1 mark)

**(b) Feedback Devices & Three-Loop Architecture — 7 marks:**

*Function of feedback devices:*
- Measure the **actual** position / velocity / current of the axis and report it to the MCU in real time.
- Enable **closed-loop control** by comparing the *commanded* value to the *actual* value, generating a *following error*, and driving the actuator to reduce that error.
- Ensure **accuracy, repeatability, and stability** of motion. (2 marks)

*Three-loop block diagram:*
- **Position loop (outer):** Compares commanded position (from interpolator) with linear-scale feedback → outputs velocity reference. Bandwidth ≈ 30–100 Hz.
- **Velocity loop (middle):** Compares velocity reference with encoder-derived velocity (or tachogenerator) → outputs current/torque reference. Bandwidth ≈ 200–500 Hz.
- **Current loop (inner):** Compares torque reference with current sensor → outputs PWM to inverter → drives motor. Bandwidth ≈ 1–4 kHz. (3 marks — labeled diagram + 1 mark per loop explanation)

*Semi-closed vs Full-closed loop:*

| Aspect | Semi-Closed | Full-Closed |
|---|---|---|
| Feedback location | On **motor shaft** (rotary encoder) | On **slide** (linear scale) |
| Compensates ball-screw error? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Accuracy | 5–10 µm typical | Sub-µm possible |
| Application | General VMC, lathe | Precision die/mold, optical | (2 marks)

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 1, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **six functional sub-systems** of a CNC machine. Describe in detail the **Automatic Tool Changer (ATC) and Tool Magazine** with reference to modern tool-capacity, identification, and chip-to-chip time. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A CNC turning operation is required to machine a shaft of diameter $D = 80$ mm from EN24 steel at cutting speed $V_c = 150$ m/min with feed per revolution $f_r = 0.30$ mm/rev and depth of cut $a_p = 2.5$ mm. Compute:
   (i) The spindle speed $N$ in rpm.
   (ii) The feed rate $F$ in mm/min.
   (iii) The Material Removal Rate (MRR) in mm³/min.
   (iv) If the available spindle has a maximum speed of 1800 rpm, comment on whether the computed $N$ is feasible and suggest a corrective action if not. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Six sub-systems & ATC — 7 marks:**

*Six sub-systems:* (1 mark each = 6, but only ~4 marks asked; list with 1-line function.)

1. **Input / Program Storage:** Punched tape → USB → Ethernet / DNC. Modern machines accept part programs via USB, network, or MT-Connect.
2. **MCU:** Brain — interpolates, decodes G/M codes, runs soft-PLC, drives servos, interfaces with HMI.
3. **Drive System:** AC brushless servo motors (typical), stepper (low-end), or hydraulic (large machines).
4. **Feedback System:** Encoders, resolvers, linear scales — close the position/velocity loop.
5. **Machine Structure:** Cast-iron / polymer-concrete bed, linear roller guides, ball screws, spindle.
6. **Auxiliary Devices:** ATC, tool magazine, pallet changer, coolant, chip conveyor, lubrication, work-holding, HMI.

*ATC and Tool Magazine — 2.5 marks:*
- **Magazine types:** Drum (small VMC, 12–24 tools), Disk/Carousel (16–40), Chain (40–240+), Matrix (very large).
- **Tool identification:** Mechanical slot coding → Optical barcode (e.g., Balluff) → RFID (modern, no contact).
- **Tool change sequence (typical VMC):** (i) Spindle orient (M19) → (ii) tool unclamp → (iii) Z-up to tool-change position → (iv) swing-arm grabs from magazine and spindle simultaneously → (v) tool exchange → (vi) swing-arm retracts → (vii) tool clamps → (viii) resume program. (1 mark)
- **Tool capacity:** 12 → 240+ tools depending on class.
- **Chip-to-chip time:** 0.5 – 1.5 s on modern VMCs; < 0.4 s on ultra-high-speed machines (e.g., Mazak Integrex, DMG MORI).
- **Tool-length & radius offsets:** Stored in the controller's offset table; activated via **G43 H##** and **G41/G42 D##**.

**(b) Numerical — 7 marks:**

**(i) Spindle speed** (2 marks)

$$
N = \dfrac{V_c \times 1000}{\pi \cdot D} = \dfrac{150 \times 1000}{\pi \times 80}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{150\,000}{251.327} = 596.83 \ \text{rpm}
$$

$$
\boxed{N \approx 597 \ \text{rpm}}
$$

**(ii) Feed rate** (1.5 marks)

$$
F = N \times f_r = 596.83 \times 0.30 = 179.05 \ \text{mm/min}
$$

$$
\boxed{F \approx 179 \ \text{mm/min}}
$$

**(iii) MRR** (1.5 marks)

For turning:

$$
\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot D \cdot f_r \cdot N \cdot a_p
$$

Wait — the standard turning MRR is $\pi \cdot D \cdot L \cdot f_r \cdot N$, where $L$ is *length of cut*. If MRR per unit length is asked, then:

$$
\text{MRR per unit length} = \pi \cdot D \cdot f_r \cdot N \cdot a_p
$$

Let us adopt the **standard form $\text{MRR} = a_p \cdot f_r \cdot V_c$**, where $V_c$ is in mm/min:

$$
V_c = 150\,000 \ \text{mm/min}
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 2.5 \times 0.30 \times 150\,000 = 112\,500 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} = 112.5 \ \text{cm}^3/\text{min}
$$

Alternatively, using the diameter form for *volume per revolution*:

$$
\text{MRR} = \pi \cdot D \cdot a_p \cdot f_r \cdot N = \pi \times 80 \times 2.5 \times 0.30 \times 596.83
$$

$$
\text{MRR} = 251.327 \times 2.5 \times 0.30 \times 596.83 \approx 112\,500 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{min}
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MRR} \approx 112\,500 \ \text{mm}^3/\text{min} \;\; (112.5 \ \text{cc/min})}
$$

**(iv) Feasibility & corrective action** (2 marks)

- Computed $N = 597$ rpm.
- Max spindle speed available = 1 800 rpm.
- Since $597 < 1800$, the spindle **can deliver the required speed comfortably** (utilization ≈ 33 %).
- No corrective action needed for feasibility.
- **Optimization suggestion (for extra credit):** The cutting speed is on the *low* side — increasing $V_c$ to ~250 m/min (recommended for coated carbide on EN24) would raise $N$ to ~995 rpm, still within limit, and **improve productivity by ~67 %**. Always consult the tool manufacturer's recommended $V_c$ chart for the chosen insert grade.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> - Forgetting to **convert $V_c$ from m/min to mm/min** before substituting (loses 0.5 mark).
> - Using **wrong MRR formula** — turning MRR ≠ milling MRR. In turning, MRR depends on $a_p$, $f_r$, and $V_c$ (or $\pi D a_p f_r N$); in milling, MRR = $a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F$.
> - **Forgetting units** on $D$ (must be mm, not m) when using $V_c$ in m/min.
> - For numerical answers, **always round to a controller-programmable value** (e.g., integer rpm, 0.1 mm precision) — examiners reward engineering judgment.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **CNC = Computer Numerical Control** — a stored part program drives a computer-based MCU which regulates multi-axis motion in real time.
- **Two classifications of CNC machine tools:**
  - *By motion control:* Point-to-Point (PTP), Straight-Cut, Contouring (2D / 2.5D / 3D / 4-axis / 5-axis).
  - *By process configuration:* Turning, Milling, Drilling, Grinding, EDM, Hybrid, Multi-tasking.
- **"Machining Center"** = CNC mill + ATC + Pallet Changer + Coolant/Chip Mgmt. — not every CNC mill qualifies.
- **Six sub-systems of a CNC machine:** Input media, MCU, Drive, Feedback, Machine structure, Auxiliaries.
- **Three-loop servo control:** Position (outer) → Velocity (middle) → Current (inner). Each inner loop must be ~10× faster than the outer for stability.
- **Open-loop (stepper):** No feedback; cheap; PTP only; stall goes undetected.
- **Closed-loop (servo):** Encoder feedback; mandatory for contouring; corrects following error.
- **Semi-closed loop:** Encoder on motor; misses ball-screw pitch error; general-purpose OK.
- **Full-closed loop:** Linear scale on slide; mandatory for < 5 µm tolerance; precision tooling.
- **MCU key blocks:** CPU, Interpolator, Soft-PLC, HMI, I/O interfaces.
- **Feedback devices:** Rotary encoder, Linear scale, Resolver, LVDT, Inductosyn, Tachogenerator.
- **DNC = Direct Numerical Control** — host computer feeds programs to CNC in real time.
- **ATC tool capacity:** 12 → 240+ tools; chip-to-chip time 0.5–1.5 s on modern VMCs.
- **Key formulas (must memorize):**
  - $V_c = \pi D N$
  - $F = N \cdot f_r$  *(turning)*
  - $F = N \cdot z \cdot f_z$  *(milling)*
  - $\text{MRR}_{\text{turning}} = a_p \cdot f_r \cdot V_c$
  - $\text{MRR}_{\text{milling}} = a_p \cdot a_e \cdot F$
  - $K_v = F / (60 \cdot E_{ss})$
- **Standards to remember:** ISO 6983 (NC programming language), ISO 841 (axis naming), ISO 2806 (machining), EIA RS-267-D (legacy), MT-Connect & OPC-UA (modern).
- **5-axis machines** must support **simultaneous 5-axis interpolation** to deliver true 5-axis contouring; "3+2" or "positional" 5-axis is *not* the same.
- **Pallet changer** = essentially zero setup time between batches → backbone of FMS.
- **Coolant trend:** High-pressure through-spindle (70+ bar) for deep-hole drilling; MQL for sustainable manufacturing.
- **Common examiner traps:**
  - Confusing *contouring* with *PTP*.
  - Confusing *semi-closed* with *full-closed* loop.
  - Forgetting unit conversions in numericals.
  - Calling a plain CNC mill a "machining center" (it isn't, without ATC).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Encoders and Interpolators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEMET866 - ADVANCED NUMERICAL CONTROL IN MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Encoders and Interpolators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Encoders — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> An **encoder** is a precision electro-mechanical/optical transducer that converts the **linear or angular position** of a moving axis into a **discrete digital (pulse) or binary-coded electrical signal**, which is fed back to the CNC controller to establish a **closed-loop position/velocity feedback** system.

In Numerical Control (NC) and CNC machinery, encoders are the "eyes" of the controller. They continuously monitor the **actual position** of slides, spindles, and rotary tables, comparing this real-time data against the **commanded position** issued by the interpolator. The difference (the **following error** $\varepsilon$) drives the servo-amplifier to correct axis motion.

### 1.1.1 Conceptual Analogy

Imagine you are blindfolded and asked to walk exactly 10 meters along a corridor. Without feedback, you would either overshoot or stop short. Now, suppose a friend counts your footsteps aloud — *one, two, three…* — and tells you when to stop. Your friend's counting is the **encoder**: each "footfall" is a **pulse**, the count gives **position**, and the rate of counting gives **velocity**. The corridor is the **machine axis**, and you are the **servo motor**.

## 1.2 Interpolators — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)**
> An **interpolator** is the computational sub-system (hardware, firmware, or software module) within the CNC controller that **generates the sequence of intermediate coordinate commands** (reference points) lying on the path connecting the start and end points of a programmed motion block, ensuring smooth, accurate, and continuous tool-path traversal.

In essence, the part program only specifies **start** and **end** coordinates of a cut (e.g., "go from $(0,0)$ to $(100, 50)$"). The interpolator "fills in" the thousands of tiny intermediate points — at sample intervals of typically **1 ms** to **4 ms** — that the servos must follow. This is the mathematical heart of every CNC controller.

### 1.2.1 Conceptual Analogy

Think of the interpolator as the **navigation system of a self-driving car**. You input a destination (end-point), and the GPS calculates every steering angle, every slight turn along the way (intermediate points) at a refresh rate of several times per second. Without the interpolator, the car would only know *where it is* and *where it should end up*, but never *how to smoothly get there*.

## 1.3 Classification Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight — Mandatory Coverage**
> 1. **Encoders:** Incremental vs. Absolute; Linear vs. Rotary; Optical vs. Magnetic
> 2. **Interpolators:** Linear (L), Circular (CW/CCW), Parabolic, Cubic, and Spline (NURBS) interpolation; Hardware (DDAS) vs. Software interpolators

The KTU 2024 scheme specifically emphasizes the **DDAS (Digital Differential Analyzer)** hardware interpolator approach, and the **mathematical formulation** of linear, circular, and parabolic interpolation since these are the building blocks of modern CNC controllers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Encoders — Detailed Classification

### 2.1.1 Incremental Encoders

An incremental encoder produces a **train of pulses** as the axis moves. Position is determined by **counting pulses from a reference (index) mark**.

- **Output:** Two square-wave channels (A and B) **90° out of phase** (quadrature), plus an optional **Index (Z or I) pulse** per revolution.
- **Resolution** is given by **lines per revolution (LPR)** or **pulses per revolution (PPR)**.
- **Quadrature decoding** multiplies the resolution by **4** (×4 decoding).

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrature Decoding — Why Two Channels?**
> Channel A and Channel B are mechanically offset by 90° electrical. By reading which channel leads the other, the controller determines **direction of motion**. By sampling on every edge of both channels, the effective resolution is quadrupled.

#### Resolution Formula

For a linear axis fitted with a linear (or "linear-scale") encoder having grating pitch $p$ (in mm) read with ×4 quadrature logic, the **linear resolution** $\delta$ is:

$$
\delta = \frac{p}{4}
$$

For a rotary encoder with $N$ lines per revolution mounted on a leadscrew of pitch $L$ (mm/rev), the effective linear resolution is:

$$
\delta = \frac{L}{4N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Loss-of-Position Hazard**
> Incremental encoders **lose absolute position** when power is cut. Upon restart, the machine must perform a **homing cycle** (referencing against a limit switch or index pulse) before resuming operation. This is a frequent KTU question theme.

### 2.1.2 Absolute Encoders

An absolute encoder outputs a **unique binary (or Gray-code) word** for every angular position. No counting is required; position is **inherently known** at power-up.

- **Output:** $n$-bit parallel word (or serial SSI/BiSS) — typically 12 to 25 bits per revolution.
- **Resolution:** $360° / 2^n$ per revolution; with multi-turn designs, additional bits encode turns.
- Uses **Gray code** to prevent multiple-bit transitions errors.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Gray Code, Not Plain Binary?**
> In Gray code, only **one bit changes** between successive positions. This eliminates transient mis-reads caused by bit-skew in parallel data lines. Example: 7 → 8 in binary is `0111 → 1000` (4 bits change), but in Gray code it is `0100 → 1100` (1 bit changes).

### 2.1.3 Linear vs. Rotary Encoders

| Feature | Linear Encoder | Rotary Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Linear displacement | Angular displacement |
| **Output element** | Optical/magnetic scale strip | Disc with radial grating |
| **Typical use** | Direct measurement on machine slides | Mounted on leadscrew or motor shaft |
| **Advantage** | Eliminates screw errors (Abbe error reduction) | Compact, easy to retrofit |
| **Resolution (typical)** | 0.1 µm to 1 µm | 10,000 to 65,536 PPR (×4 quadrature) |

## 2.2 Interpolators — Mathematical Foundation

### 2.2.1 Linear Interpolation (G01)

Linear interpolation moves the tool along a **straight line** from a start point $P_1 = (x_1, y_1, z_1)$ to an end point $P_2 = (x_2, y_2, z_2)$ at a **programmed feed rate $F$** (e.g., mm/min).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Feed-Rate Decomposition**
> The interpolator's principal job is to enforce the **vector feed rate $F$** along the path, not the individual axis rates. For a 2D linear block of length $L$, the axis feed components are:
> $$F_x = F \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{L}, \quad F_y = F \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{L}$$

For 3-axis linear motion the general parametric form (with $u \in [0, 1]$) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(u) &= x_1 + u \, (x_2 - x_1) \\
y(u) &= y_1 + u \, (y_2 - y_1) \\
z(u) &= z_1 + u \, (z_2 - z_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.2.2 Circular Interpolation (G02 / G03)

Circular interpolation commands an arc on a **circle in a specified plane (XY, YZ, or XZ)** with given center $I, J, K$ (or radius $R$), and a **direction** (CW = G02 or CCW = G03).

**Parametric form** of a circle in the XY plane, center $(x_c, y_c)$, radius $R$, with $\theta$ varying from $\theta_s$ to $\theta_e$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\theta) &= x_c + R \cos \theta \\
y(\theta) &= y_c + R \sin \theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Arc-length feed-rate constraint:** at each sample, the angular velocity $\dot{\theta}$ must satisfy:

$$
R \, \dot{\theta} = F \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \dot{\theta} = \frac{F}{R}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall (KTU valuation):** When the arc center is specified using **R (radius) instead of I, J, K**, the controller automatically chooses the **minor arc** (≤ 180°) by default, unless explicitly programmed as a full circle. Students frequently lose marks by not stating this convention.

### 2.2.3 Parabolic and Higher-Order Interpolation

> [!NOTE]
> **Why higher-order interpolation?**
> Linear and circular blocks joined end-to-end produce **discontinuous curvature** at the joints, causing accelerations spikes and poor surface finish on high-speed dies, moulds, and aerospace free-form surfaces. Parabolic and cubic interpolators (and ultimately **NURBS / B-spline interpolation**) maintain $G^2$ or $G^3$ continuity of curvature.

A **parabolic** (quadratic) blend uses a parabola of the form:

$$
y = a x^2 + b x + c
$$

fitted through three points (entry, junction, exit) so that slopes match at both ends — yielding **continuous tangent (G¹ continuity)**.

**Cubic spline interpolation** uses piecewise cubic polynomials ensuring continuity of position, slope, and curvature at every knot — i.e., $C^2$ / $G^2$ continuity.

## 2.3 Hardware vs. Software Interpolators

| Type | Mechanism | Speed | Resolution | Used in |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **DA (Digital-to-Analog) interpolator** | Uses analog rate multipliers and integrators | Very fast | Limited by analog drift | Older NC systems (1970s) |
| **DDAS (Digital Differential Analyzer)** | Hardware integrator using binary up/down counters | Fast (µs-level) | High (limited by clock) | Mid-era CNC, Fanuc early systems |
| **Software (firmware) interpolator** | Real-time algorithm in the controller CPU/MCU | Software-bound, but flexible | Arbitrarily high (limited by sample time $T_s$) | All modern CNC (Fanuc 30i, Siemens 840D, Heidenhain TNC) |

### 2.4 DDAS — The Hardware Interpolator in Detail

The **Digital Differential Analyzer (DDA)** is the work-horse hardware interpolator of KTU syllabus. It uses two **binary integrators**, one per axis, to convert a **digital velocity command** into **incremental position pulses**.

**Operating principle for linear interpolation in 2 axes:**

Let $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ be the integrated pulse counts needed for the block. The DDA computes the ratio $\Delta y / \Delta x$ by repeatedly adding $\Delta y$ to a Y-register and $\Delta x$ to an X-register, with the axis that **overflows first** emitting a pulse. The path traced is a **DDA-integrated straight line**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite Result**
> In a DDA of $n$-bit registers with $\Delta x$ loaded into the X-integrator, the **frequency ratio** of the X-axis pulse train to the iteration clock is:
> $$f_x = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{2^n}$$
> This is the core of the **hardware digital integration** that the syllabus demands.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Quick-Reference Table — Memorize before exams**

| # | Quantity / Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Linear resolution (×4 quadrature, linear scale) | $\delta = p / 4$ | mm | $p$ = grating pitch |
| 2 | Linear resolution (rotary encoder on screw) | $\delta = L / (4 N)$ | mm | $L$ = screw pitch, $N$ = LPR |
| 3 | Angular resolution (absolute rotary) | $\theta_{\text{res}} = 360° / 2^n$ | degrees | $n$ = number of bits |
| 4 | Linear interpolator – param eq. | $x(u) = x_1 + u \Delta x$ | mm | $u \in [0, 1]$ |
| 5 | Vector feed decomposition | $F_x = F \cdot \Delta x / L$ | mm/min | $L$ = block length |
| 6 | Block length (2D) | $L = \sqrt{\Delta x^2 + \Delta y^2}$ | mm | 3D adds $\Delta z^2$ |
| 7 | Circular param eq. (XY) | $x = x_c + R\cos\theta$, $y = y_c + R\sin\theta$ | mm | $\theta$ sweep = arc range |
| 8 | Arc angular velocity (feed constraint) | $\dot{\theta} = F / R$ | rad/min | Const $F$ ⇒ const $\dot{\theta}$ |
| 9 | DDA pulse rate | $f_x = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \Delta x / 2^n$ | pulses/s | $n$ = register bits |
| 10 | Following error (servo) | $\varepsilon = x_{\text{cmd}} - x_{\text{act}}$ | mm | Drives servo correction |
| 11 | Velocity from encoder | $v = f_{\text{pulse}} \cdot \delta$ | mm/s | $f_{\text{pulse}}$ in pulses/s |
| 12 | Parabola fit (3 points) | $y = a x^2 + b x + c$ | — | Continuity of slope at joints |
| 13 | Cubic spline | $S_i(x) = a_i + b_i x + c_i x^2 + d_i x^3$ | — | $C^2$ continuity at knots |
| 14 | Maximum contouring error | $e_{\max} \approx v^2 / (2 a_{\max})$ | mm | Function of accel $a$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility**
> The encoder-interpolator-servo chain is the **backbone of every precision CNC machine** — from 3-axis VMCs and 5-axis machining centres to semiconductor lithography stages and robotic welding arms. Mastering these formulae directly maps to **CO2 (apply position-feedback concepts)** and **CO3 (analyze interpolation algorithms)** of the KTU 2024 syllabus.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linear Interpolation Feed Decomposition

**Problem setup:** The part program issues `G01 X100 Y60 F300` (linear block from origin to $(100, 60)$ mm at $F = 300$ mm/min). Derive the per-axis feed rates $F_x$ and $F_y$.

**Step 1 — Compute total path length $L$:**

The straight-line distance between $(0, 0)$ and $(100, 60)$ is:

$$
L = \sqrt{(x_2 - x_1)^2 + (y_2 - y_1)^2} = \sqrt{(100 - 0)^2 + (60 - 0)^2} = \sqrt{10000 + 3600}
$$

$$
L = \sqrt{13600} = 116.6190 \text{ mm (approx.)}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute direction cosines (unit vector along path):**

$$
\cos\alpha = \frac{\Delta x}{L} = \frac{100}{116.6190} = 0.8575
$$

$$
\cos\beta = \frac{\Delta y}{L} = \frac{60}{116.6190} = 0.5145
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply by feed rate $F$ to get axis feeds:**

$$
F_x = F \cdot \cos\alpha = 300 \cdot 0.8575 = 257.246 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
F_y = F \cdot \cos\beta = 300 \cdot 0.5145 = 154.354 \text{ mm/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the resultant matches programmed feed:**

$$
\sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2} = \sqrt{257.246^2 + 154.354^2} = \sqrt{66175.5 + 23825.2} = \sqrt{90000.7} \approx 300 \text{ mm/min} \;\checkmark
$$

This confirms the interpolator correctly decomposes the vector feed into two synchronized axis feeds, both of which must begin and end **simultaneously** to maintain the straight-line trajectory.

## 3.2 Derivation: Circular Interpolation Centre and Quadrant Tracking

**Problem setup:** Program a CCW arc (G03) from $(10, 0)$ to $(0, 10)$ with center $I = 0, J = 10$ (i.e., centre at $(0, 10)$) and $F = 200$ mm/min. Derive the parametric trajectory and the angular velocity command.

**Step 1 — Determine radius and sweep angle:**

The radius is the distance from centre $(0, 10)$ to the start point $(10, 0)$:

$$
R = \sqrt{(10 - 0)^2 + (0 - 10)^2} = \sqrt{100 + 100} = \sqrt{200} = 14.1421 \text{ mm}
$$

The start angle $\theta_s$ (measured from centre, with X-axis as reference) is:

$$
\theta_s = \arctan\!\left(\frac{y_s - y_c}{x_s - x_c}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{0 - 10}{10 - 0}\right) = \arctan(-1) = -45°
$$

The end angle $\theta_e$ at point $(0, 10)$ is:

$$
\theta_e = \arctan\!\left(\frac{10 - 10}{0 - 0}\right) = 90° \quad (\text{on the +Y axis})
$$

The CCW sweep is therefore from $-45°$ to $+90°$, total $\Delta\theta = 135° = 2.3562$ rad.

**Step 2 — Parametric equations of the path:**

For any $\theta \in [-45°, 90°]$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
x(\theta) &= 0 + R \cos\theta = 14.1421 \cos\theta \\
y(\theta) &= 10 + R \sin\theta = 10 + 14.1421 \sin\theta
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute angular velocity to enforce feed rate:**

The constraint is that the **tangential velocity** along the arc equals $F$:

$$
v_{\text{tan}} = R \cdot \dot{\theta} = F
$$

$$
\dot{\theta} = \frac{F}{R} = \frac{200 \text{ mm/min}}{14.1421 \text{ mm}} = 14.1421 \text{ rad/min}
$$

Equivalently in revolutions per minute:

$$
N = \frac{\dot{\theta}}{2\pi} = \frac{14.1421}{6.2832} = 2.2508 \text{ rev/min}
$$

**Step 4 — Total time to complete the arc:**

$$
t = \frac{\Delta\theta}{\dot{\theta}} = \frac{2.3562 \text{ rad}}{14.1421 \text{ rad/min}} = 0.1666 \text{ min} = 10.0 \text{ s}
$$

**Cross-check via arc length:**

Arc length $s = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 14.1421 \times 2.3562 = 33.333$ mm. Time $= s / F = 33.333 / 200 = 0.1667$ min $\approx 10$ s. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: DDA (Digital Differential Analyzer) Linear Interpolation

The DDA is the canonical hardware interpolator. For a 2-axis linear block from $(0, 0)$ to $(\Delta x_{\max}, \Delta y_{\max})$, the DDA executes an iterative loop. Below is the rigorous derivation of the **overflow-based pulse-generation principle**.

**Setup:**

- Two $n$-bit binary up-counters (integrators) $X$ and $Y$.
- Registers loaded with $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ respectively (both $\le 2^n - 1$).
- An iteration clock of period $T_{\text{clk}}$ drives the loop.

**Step 1 — Per-iteration increment:**

Each clock tick, the X-integrator is incremented by $\Delta x$ (mod $2^n$), and the Y-integrator by $\Delta y$ (mod $2^n$). The increment per iteration is the digital "rate."

**Step 2 — Overflow detection:**

If a register rolls over past $2^n - 1$, it produces a **carry-out** (overflow) signal. That signal is used to **emit a position pulse** to the corresponding axis servo.

**Step 3 — Derive pulse frequency:**

Out of every $2^n$ clock ticks, the X-integrator will overflow exactly $\Delta x$ times (on average) — because each tick adds $\Delta x$ to the register, and $2^n / \Delta x$ ticks are required to accumulate $2^n$ and overflow. So the **average pulse rate** is:

$$
f_x = \frac{\text{overflows per second}}{1} = \frac{1}{T_{\text{clk}}} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{2^n} = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{2^n}
$$

**Step 4 — Velocity ratio = slope of line:**

Similarly:

$$
f_y = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{2^n}
$$

Therefore the **ratio of emitted axis pulses** equals the **slope of the commanded line**:

$$
\frac{f_y}{f_x} = \frac{\Delta y}{\Delta x} = \text{slope}
$$

**Step 5 — Total pulses emitted over the block:**

Integrate over the block duration $T_{\text{block}}$:

$$
\text{Total X pulses} = f_x \cdot T_{\text{block}} = \Delta x_{\text{axis}}
$$

This confirms the DDA correctly delivers the commanded displacement on each axis, in the correct ratio, and consequently along a straight line.

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantization error of the DDA:**
> Because $\Delta x$ and $\Delta y$ are integers, the DDA-traced line is **stair-stepped** at very fine scale — the "staircase" being a numerical artefact. For a 16-bit DDA, the maximum quantization error on either axis is $\pm 1$ LSB, and the radius error on a circular DDA is on the order of $\pm 1$ LSB per quadrant.

## 3.4 Derivation: Circular DDA — Quadrant Logic

For a circular arc, the DDAS uses **two integrators plus an "end-of-arc" detector** based on quadrant transitions. The derivation:

- Load $X$ register with $|x_0|$ (start $x$-offset) and $Y$ register with $|y_0|$ (start $y$-offset) from the arc center.
- Each iteration: decrement the **major-axis** register by $\Delta_{\text{maj}}$ and increment the **minor-axis** register by $\Delta_{\text{min}}$, where:

$$
\Delta_{\text{maj}} = \left| \frac{x_0}{R_0} \right|, \quad \Delta_{\text{min}} = \left| \frac{y_0}{R_0} \right|
$$

with $R_0 = \sqrt{x_0^2 + y_0^2}$ the radius. The overflow of the major-axis register signals a **quadrant transition**; at this instant the role of major/minor axes is swapped, ensuring the trajectory stays on the true circle within $\pm 1$ LSB.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Interpolation Comparison

| Criterion | Linear (G01) | Circular (G02/G03) | Parabolic (G06.2 / spline) | Cubic / NURBS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Mathematical order** | 1st | 2nd (radius) | 2nd (quadratic) | 3rd (cubic) |
| **Continuity at block boundaries** | Position only ($C^0$) | Position + tangent ($G^1$) | $G^1$ | $G^2$ (curvature) |
| **Feed-rate constraint** | Constant $F$ | Constant $F$ (auto $\dot\theta$) | Constant $F$ | Constant $F$ + accel limits |
| **Computation** | Trivial | Trig or DDA | Quadratic solution | Tridiagonal spline solve |
| **Hardware support** | All CNC | All CNC | Selected controllers | Modern high-end (840D, 30i) |
| **Typical application** | Straight cuts | Holes, fillets | Die entry/exit | Free-form surfaces, moulds |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
> Differentiate between an **incremental encoder** and an **absolute encoder**. Mention one application scenario where each is preferred.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Pulses (A, B, Z) | Unique binary / Gray code word |
| Position on power-up | Unknown (homing required) | Instantly known |
| Resolution | Determined by LPR & ×4 decoding | $360°/2^n$ |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Wiring | Simpler (3–4 wires) | Complex (parallel) or serial (SSI) |
| Drift over time | No (just count) | No (inherently absolute) |

**Preferred applications:**
- **Incremental:** Lead-screws of conventional 3-axis VMCs where cost is critical and homing cycle is acceptable.
- **Absolute:** Rotary tables, turrets, and robotic joints where loss of position on power failure is unsafe (e.g., CNC tool-changer, surgical robots).

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
> What is **quadrature decoding** in an incremental encoder? Why is the effective resolution multiplied by 4?

**Model Answer:**

Quadrature decoding uses two square-wave channels (A and B) that are 90° out of phase to extract **direction of motion** and **four-fold resolution**. The effective resolution is multiplied by 4 because the counter can be triggered on **all four edges** of the combined waveform — the rising and falling edges of channel A, and the rising and falling edges of channel B. Hence an encoder with 2500 LPR provides $2500 \times 4 = 10000$ counts/revolution. Direction is determined by checking which channel's edge is encountered first.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Question A (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
>
> **(a)** A CNC milling machine uses an **incremental rotary encoder** of **5000 lines/revolution** mounted on a **ballscrew of 10 mm pitch**. The encoder is read with **×4 quadrature logic**. Compute:
> 1. The **linear resolution** per count (in µm).
> 2. The **total number of feedback counts** generated when the table moves **exactly 250 mm**.
> 3. The **maximum following error** allowable (in counts) if the controller tolerance band is $\pm 5$ µm.
>
> **(b)** Explain with a neat block diagram the **DDAS (Digital Differential Analyzer) hardware interpolator** for 2-axis linear interpolation. Derive the relation between the **output pulse frequency** and the **commanded axis displacement**.

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Linear resolution per count:**

$$
\delta = \frac{L_{\text{pitch}}}{4 \times N_{\text{LPR}}} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{4 \times 5000} = \frac{10}{20000} = 0.0005 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ µm}
$$

2. **Total counts for 250 mm:**

$$
\text{Counts} = \frac{250 \text{ mm}}{0.0005 \text{ mm/count}} = 500{,}000 \text{ counts}
$$

3. **Maximum following error in counts (for ± 5 µm):**

$$
\varepsilon_{\max} = \frac{5 \text{ µm}}{0.5 \text{ µm/count}} = 10 \text{ counts}
$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Refer to the **DDA block diagram** in Section 4.3. The key points to mention in the answer are:

- The DDA consists of two **n-bit binary integrators** (one per axis) and an **iteration clock**.
- Each clock tick adds $\Delta x$ to the X-register and $\Delta y$ to the Y-register (mod $2^n$).
- An **overflow** of the X-register produces an X-pulse to the X-axis servo; same for Y.
- The relation derived is:

$$
f_x = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{2^n}, \qquad f_y = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{2^n}
$$

- The **ratio $f_y / f_x = \Delta y / \Delta x$** is the **slope of the commanded straight line**, ensuring the trajectory is geometrically correct.
- The total pulses emitted per block equal the commanded axis displacements: $\Delta x_{\text{axis}}$ and $\Delta y_{\text{axis}}$.

> **Question B (14 marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
>
> **(a)** A part program contains the block `G01 X80 Y60 F400` issued from the current tool position $(0, 0)$. Compute:
> 1. The **block length** $L$.
> 2. The **axis feed components** $F_x$ and $F_y$.
> 3. The **time** to complete the block.
>
> **(b)** With a neat block diagram, describe the **circular interpolation** algorithm in 2 axes (XY plane). For a CCW arc (G03) from $(20, 0)$ to $(0, 20)$ about the centre $(0, 0)$, with $F = 300$ mm/min, compute the **angular velocity** $\dot\theta$ and the **time** to complete the arc.

**Model Solution (a):**

1. **Block length:**

$$
L = \sqrt{80^2 + 60^2} = \sqrt{6400 + 3600} = \sqrt{10000} = 100 \text{ mm}
$$

2. **Axis feeds:**

$$
F_x = F \cdot \frac{\Delta x}{L} = 400 \cdot \frac{80}{100} = 320 \text{ mm/min}
$$

$$
F_y = F \cdot \frac{\Delta y}{L} = 400 \cdot \frac{60}{100} = 240 \text{ mm/min}
$$

3. **Block time:**

$$
t = \frac{L}{F} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{400 \text{ mm/min}} = 0.25 \text{ min} = 15 \text{ s}
$$

**Model Solution (b):**

Refer to the **Functional Architecture** diagram (Section 4.4) and the parametric equations in Section 2.2.2.

- **Radius:**

$$
R = \sqrt{20^2 + 0^2} = 20 \text{ mm}
$$

- **Start angle:** $\theta_s = 0°$ (point on +X axis).
- **End angle:** $\theta_e = 90°$ (point on +Y axis).
- **Sweep:** $\Delta\theta = 90° = \pi/2$ rad $= 1.5708$ rad.

- **Angular velocity:**

$$
\dot\theta = \frac{F}{R} = \frac{300 \text{ mm/min}}{20 \text{ mm}} = 15 \text{ rad/min}
$$

- **Time to complete arc:**

$$
t = \frac{\Delta\theta}{\dot\theta} = \frac{1.5708 \text{ rad}}{15 \text{ rad/min}} = 0.1047 \text{ min} = 6.283 \text{ s}
$$

- **Cross-check (arc length method):** $s = R \cdot \Delta\theta = 20 \times 1.5708 = 31.416$ mm. Time $= 31.416 / 300 = 0.1047$ min $\approx 6.28$ s. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the ×4 factor** in quadrature resolution — most common single error; loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Mixing up direction conventions** for G02 (CW) vs. G03 (CCW) — the KTU valuation key will specifically check the direction based on the centre specified.
> 3. **Stating feed rate $F$ in mm/s when the program uses mm/min** (or vice versa) — always confirm units.
> 4. **Linear-interpolation answer missing the direction-cosine derivation** — writing only $F_x = F \cos\alpha$ without deriving $\cos\alpha = \Delta x / L$ loses full method marks.
> 5. **DDA answer without the overflow-modulo logic** — marks are awarded for explaining *how* the register overflow produces a pulse.
> 6. **Ignoring the I, J, K convention** for circular interpolation centres (I = X-offset, J = Y-offset, K = Z-offset) — the I/J values are **signed**, not always positive.
> 7. **Not mentioning Gray code** when describing absolute encoders — easy 1-mark addition that is often missed.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Encoders and Interpolators**

### Encoders
- **Incremental encoder:** Pulse-based; needs homing; cheap; ×4 quadrature boosts resolution.
- **Absolute encoder:** Unique word per position; survives power-cycle; uses **Gray code** to avoid bit-skew.
- **Linear encoder** = position scale; **rotary encoder** = angular scale.
- Resolution formulae: $\delta = p/4$ (linear scale) and $\delta = L/(4N)$ (rotary on screw).
- Angular resolution of $n$-bit absolute: $360°/2^n$.

### Interpolators
- **Linear (G01):** parametric $x(u) = x_1 + u \Delta x$; feed decomposition via direction cosines.
- **Circular (G02 CW, G03 CCW):** parametric $(x_c + R\cos\theta,\, y_c + R\sin\theta)$; angular velocity $\dot\theta = F/R$.
- **Parabolic / Cubic / Spline:** used for $G^1$ and $G^2$ continuity, free-form surfaces, NURBS.
- **DA interpolator:** Analog; obsolete; uses integrators and rate multipliers.
- **DDAS interpolator:** Hardware digital; two $n$-bit integrators; overflow → axis pulse; relation $f_x = f_{\text{clk}} \cdot \Delta x / 2^n$.
- **Software interpolator:** Modern CNC; runs in real-time on the controller CPU at sample period $T_s$.

### Key KTU Buzz-Words to Use in Answers
- Closed-loop feedback, following error, position loop, velocity loop, current loop.
- Quadrature decoding, Gray code, index pulse, homing cycle.
- DDA, overflow, modulo arithmetic, direction cosine, vector feed rate.
- G-code (G00, G01, G02, G03), block length, I/J/K, R (radius).
- $C^0$ / $C^1$ / $C^2$ / $G^1$ / $G^2$ continuity.

### Numerical Conversion Handy-Rules
