# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Effective Linear Resolution of a CNC Axis

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU Numerical):**
A CNC milling machine has a ball-screw with a **lead of 10 mm/rev**. The servo motor is fitted with an **incremental rotary encoder having 2500 lines/rev**. The encoder output is counted in **quadrature (×4) decoding**. Calculate:
1. The basic pulse count per revolution.
2. The effective linear resolution at the machine table.
3. The frequency of the encoder pulse train when the slide moves at a feed of **6000 mm/min**.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Basic pulse count per revolution.**

The encoder has **N = 2500** grating lines on its disc. Each line passing the photo-detector produces one electrical pulse per channel. Hence:

$$N_{basic} = 2500 \text{ pulses/rev}$$

**Step 2 — Quadrature-decoded pulse count.**

In quadrature decoding, both edges of Channel A and Channel B are counted, multiplying the basic count by **m = 4**:

$$N_{eff} = m \cdot N = 4 \times 2500 = 10{,}000 \text{ counts/rev}$$

**Step 3 — Linear displacement of the slide per motor revolution.**

The ball-screw converts one motor revolution into a linear table movement equal to the lead:

$$L = 10 \text{ mm/rev}$$

**Step 4 — Effective linear resolution.**

This is the smallest linear table movement that produces one countable pulse:

$$\Delta x = \frac{L}{N_{eff}} = \frac{10 \text{ mm}}{10{,}000 \text{ counts}} = 0.001 \text{ mm/count} = 1\ \mu\text{m/count}$$

**Step 5 — Pulse-train frequency at 6000 mm/min feed.**

First, find the motor speed corresponding to the linear feedrate:

$$n = \frac{V_f}{L} = \frac{6000 \text{ mm/min}}{10 \text{ mm/rev}} = 600 \text{ rev/min} = 10 \text{ rev/s}$$

The pulse frequency in quadrature is:

$$f = n \cdot N_{eff} = 10 \text{ rev/s} \times 10{,}000 \text{ counts/rev} = 100{,}000 \text{ Hz} = 100 \text{ kHz}$$

**Result:** Resolution = **1 µm/count**, Pulse frequency = **100 kHz**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — LVDT Output Voltage and Sensitivity

**Problem Statement:**
An LVDT has a primary excited by **5 V rms at 5 kHz**. The turns ratio (primary : each secondary) is **1 : 0.5**. The core stroke is **±25 mm**. Calculate:
1. The output voltage when the core is displaced by **+10 mm** from the null.
2. The sensitivity constant **K**.
3. The RMS output if both secondaries are individually measured.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Mutually induced EMFs in the secondaries.**

The induced EMF in each secondary, by transformer action, is:

$$e_s = E_p \cdot \frac{N_s}{N_p}$$

$$e_s = 5 \text{ V} \times 0.5 = 2.5 \text{ V rms (each secondary at maximum coupling)}$$

**Step 2 — Differential connection.**

The two secondaries are connected in series opposition. Let the core displacement be **x** from the null. The coupling to the upper secondary increases linearly with x, while the coupling to the lower secondary decreases. Their individual secondary EMFs are:

$$e_{s1} = 2.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{x}{L}\right) \text{ V}$$

$$e_{s2} = 2.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{x}{L}\right) \text{ V}$$

where **L = 50 mm** is the total core stroke length (from -25 to +25).

**Step 3 — Differential output voltage.**

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = 2.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{x}{L}\right) - 2.5 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{2} - \frac{x}{L}\right) = 2.5 \cdot \frac{2x}{L}$$

Simplifying:

$$e_o = \frac{5x}{L}$$

**Step 4 — Sensitivity constant K.**

$$K = \frac{e_o}{x} = \frac{5 \text{ V}}{50 \text{ mm}} = 0.1 \text{ V/mm}$$

**Step 5 — Output at x = +10 mm.**

$$e_o = 0.1 \times 10 = 1.0 \text{ V rms}$$

**Result:** Output at 10 mm = **1.0 V rms**, Sensitivity **K = 100 mV/mm**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Following Error and Servo Velocity Gain

**Problem Statement:**
A CNC axis has a servo loop with velocity gain **$K_v = 30\ \text{s}^{-1}$**. The programmed feedrate is **$V_f = 9000\ \text{mm/min}$**. Compute the steady-state following (lag) error of the slide.

### Step-by-Step Working

**Step 1 — Convert feedrate to consistent units.**

$$V_f = \frac{9000 \text{ mm/min}}{60} = 150 \text{ mm/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the standard velocity-error formula for a Type-1 servo.**

For a CNC axis with proportional + integral position control (Type-1 system), the steady-state position error at constant feedrate is:

$$E = \frac{V_f}{K_v}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values.**

$$E = \frac{150 \text{ mm/s}}{30\ \text{s}^{-1}} = 5.0 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** The slide lags the commanded position by **5 mm** during a constant-velocity move. The integral gain term reduces this lag to zero in steady state; hence production CNC machines always use **PI or PID** control, never purely P.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Key Feedback Devices

| Property | Incremental Encoder | Absolute Encoder | Resolver | LVDT | Linear Glass Scale |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output type | Pulse train | Digital word (Gray) | Analog sin/cos | Analog differential | Pulse train / 1 Vpp |
| Resolution | Up to 0.018° | Up to 29-bit (0.0000017°) | 14–16 bit (R/D) | ~0.1 µm | 1 nm – 0.1 µm |
| Robustness | Medium (glass disc) | Medium | Very high (no electronics) | High (sealed) | High (sealed) |
| Survives oil/dirt | Limited | Limited | Excellent | Excellent | Good (with cover) |
| Cost | Low | High | Medium | Medium–High | High |
| Typical use in CNC | Servo motor feedback | Spindle C-axis indexing | Spindle / harsh axes | Tool setter, hydraulic | Direct position on table |
| Power-loss behaviour | Needs homing | Position retained | Needs homing | Needs homing | Needs homing |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — CNC Axis Closed-Loop with Sensor Feedback

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the canonical flow of command and feedback signals in a single CNC axis. Note that all node IDs begin with a letter prefix and contain only alphanumeric characters, in compliance with the engine's Mermaid safeguards.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Part Program G-Code] --> A2[CNC Controller Interpolator]
    A2 --> A3[Position Command Pulses Xref]
    A3 --> A4[PID Position Controller]
    A4 --> A5[Servo Amplifier]
    A5 --> A6[Servo Motor]
    A6 --> A7[Ball Screw]
    A7 --> A8[Machine Slide Table]

    A8 -. Actual Tool Position .-> A9[Linear Glass Scale Direct Measuring]
    A6 -. Motor Shaft Angle .-> A10[Rotary Incremental Encoder Indirect Measuring]
    A9 --> A11[Signal Conditioner and Counter]
    A10 --> A11
    A11 --> A12[Feedback Position Xact]
    A12 --> A13[Error Detector Epsilon equals Xref minus Xact]
    A13 --> A4

    A14[Spindle Speed Sensor Resolver] --> A15[Spindle Controller]
    A16[Tool Length Probe] --> A2
    A17[Spindle Load Cell] --> A18[Adaptive Control Module]
    A18 --> A5
    A19[Thermocouple on Lead Screw] --> A20[Thermal Error Compensation Block]
    A20 --> A4
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R0[CNC Sensors and Feedback Devices] --> R1[Position Sensors]

    R1 --> R1A[Linear Position]
    R1 --> R1B[Rotary Position]

    R1A --> R1A2[Linear Glass Scale]
    R1A --> R1A3[Linear Inductosyn]

    R2 --> R2A[DC Tachogenerator]
    R2 --> R2B[AC Tachogenerator]

    R3 --> R3A[Touch Trigger Probe]

```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — Signal Conditioning of a CNC Position Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Raw Sensor Output Pulses or Analog] --> S2[Amplifier and Buffer]
    S2 --> S3[Filter Anti aliasing]
    S3 --> S4[Analog to Digital Conversion for Resolver and LVDT]
    S3 --> S5[Quadrature Decoder for Incremental Encoder]
    S4 --> S6[Microcontroller or FPGA Counter]
    S5 --> S6
    S6 --> S7[Position Word to CNC Controller]
    S6 --> S8[Velocity Word d theta over dt]
    S7 --> S9[Error Calculator inside CNC]
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[PID Output to Servo Drive]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Signal Conditioner** is the unsung hero of CNC feedback. A noisy or unfiltered encoder signal will produce spurious position counts, causing the slide to vibrate ("hunting") or even crash into the workpiece. Industrial-grade CNC cabinets always include **EMI shielding, optical isolation and differential line receivers (RS-422 / 26LS32)** for all encoder wiring.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

**Define a transducer. Differentiate between an active and a passive transducer with one CNC-relevant example of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **transducer** is a device that converts one form of physical energy into another. In CNC machines, the input is generally a non-electrical quantity (position, force, temperature) and the output is an electrical signal (voltage, current, or frequency).

| Feature | Active Transducer | Passive Transducer |
|---|---|---|
| Energy source | Self-generating | Requires external excitation |
| Output without excitation | Yes | No |
| CNC Example 1 | Piezoelectric force sensor on the tool holder | LVDT on a tool-length setter |
| CNC Example 2 | Thermocouple on spindle bearing | Strain-gauge load cell on ball-screw nut |

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Award 1 mark for the definition, 1 mark for the correct differentiation table, and 1 mark for valid CNC examples. A common mistake is writing "LVDT" as an active transducer — it is **passive** because it needs an AC excitation.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**With a neat sketch, explain the working of an optical incremental encoder used for position feedback in CNC machines.**

**Model Answer:**

An **optical incremental encoder** consists of four main parts:

1. **LED Light Source** — emits a collimated beam of light.
2. **Code Disc** — a glass or metal disc ruled with **N** equally-spaced radial grating lines (transparent / opaque pattern).
3. **Photodetector (Photodiode / Phototransistor)** — receives the modulated light.
4. **Signal Processing Electronics** — amplifies and shapes the pulses into square-wave TTL/HTL outputs.

**Working Principle:** As the disc rotates with the motor shaft, the grating lines alternately pass and block the light beam. This produces a train of electrical pulses whose **frequency is proportional to the shaft speed** and whose **pulse count is proportional to the angular displacement**.

A second photodetector, mechanically offset by 90° (one-quarter pitch), produces the **Channel B** signal in quadrature with Channel A, allowing the controller to determine **direction of rotation** (A leads B for clockwise, B leads A for anti-clockwise). A third, single-line track provides the **Index (Z) pulse** exactly once per revolution for homing the axis.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Cue:** Always label the **LED, disc, photodetector, and the Channel A / B / Index signals** in your sketch. Most students forget the **index channel** and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In the KTU 2024 End-Semester Examination, every Part B question carries **14 marks** and offers an **internal choice** (either Q5(a) or Q5(b) from a Module). Both choices must be attempted in your answer book — but you write the answer for only ONE of the two alternatives, as instructed by the question paper.

---

### Question 5(A) — [14 Marks, KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]

**(a) Explain with neat sketches the various position feedback devices used in CNC machines. Compare their performance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The following position feedback devices are used in CNC machines:

1. **Optical Incremental Encoder** — Already described above; provides pulse train proportional to rotation; resolution = 4N counts/rev in quadrature.

2. **Absolute Optical Encoder** — Multi-track disc with N-bit Gray code; the entire position word is available at every instant; no homing required after power-off; used on **spindle C-axis** for indexing.

3. **Resolver** — Rugged analog rotary transformer with rotor and two stator windings 90° apart; outputs sin θ and cos θ modulated on a carrier; survives vibration, oil and high temperature; ideal for **spindle feedback**.

4. **LVDT** — Linear passive inductive transducer; gives analog differential output proportional to core displacement; used for **tool-length measurement** and **hydraulic ram position**.

5. **Linear Glass / Metal Scale** — Direct measuring system; the read-head rides on the slide and counts graduations on a fixed scale; **eliminates ball-screw errors**; used on precision machining centres and CNC lathes.

6. **Inductosyn** — Planar multi-pole transformer with 2 mm pitch on the slider/scale; gives sub-micron accuracy; used in ultra-precision machines.

**Performance Comparison (3 marks for the comparative table):**

| Device | Resolution | Robustness | Cost | Homing needed |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Incremental Encoder | 0.018° | Medium | Low | Yes |
| Absolute Encoder | 0.0000017° | Medium | High | No |
| Resolver | 0.01° | Very High | Medium | Yes |
| LVDT | 0.1 µm | High | Medium | Yes |
| Linear Glass Scale | 0.1 µm – 1 nm | High | Very High | Yes |

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, derive the output equation of an LVDT. List its advantages and disadvantages for CNC applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

*(See Section 3.2 for the full derivation. The student must draw the LVDT cross-section with: primary P, two secondaries S1 and S2 wound in opposition, the movable core, and the AC excitation source. The differential output is computed as:)*

$$e_o = e_{s1} - e_{s2} = K \cdot x$$

where

$$K = \frac{2 \cdot E_p \cdot (N_s / N_p)}{L}$$

**Advantages of LVDT (any 4 for 2 marks):**

1. **Frictionless measurement** — the core does not touch the windings, so there is no wear.
2. **Infinite resolution** — being an analog device, it is limited only by the resolution of the demodulating voltmeter.
3. **High linearity** within the central 50 % of the stroke.
4. **Robust construction** — sealed, oil- and dust-resistant; survives harsh CNC shop-floor conditions.
5. **Bidirectional output** — phase of output voltage reverses with direction of core motion, giving direction discrimination.

**Disadvantages of LVDT (any 3 for 1.5 marks):**

1. Requires a stable **AC excitation** source and a **phase-sensitive demodulator** — increases electronic complexity.
2. The output is affected by **temperature drift** of the carrier frequency and amplitude.
3. Limited **dynamic response** compared to digital encoders.
4. **Stray capacitance** between windings can introduce error at long cable lengths.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> The single most common mistake students make is writing the LVDT output equation as $e_o = e_{s1} + e_{s2}$ (series-aiding). Because the two secondaries are connected in **series-opposition (anti-series)**, the correct combination is **subtraction**. Any answer that uses a plus sign will lose **2 marks outright**.

---

### Question 5(B) — [14 Marks, KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a) Describe the construction and working of a resolver. Show how it is used for position feedback in a CNC servo loop with a block diagram. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Construction:**
A **resolver** is a precision rotary transformer with:

- A **rotor** (single winding, excited by an AC carrier of frequency 5–10 kHz).
- A **stator** with two identical windings displaced mechanically by **90°** (one aligned with the rotor's axis of symmetry, the other orthogonal).
- A shaft that couples the rotor to the motor or spindle.

**Working Principle:**

The rotor is energised by a sinusoidal carrier $V_r = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t)$. By transformer action, the two stator windings induce:

$$V_{s1}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \sin\theta$$
$$V_{s2}(t) = V_0 \sin(\omega_c t) \cdot \cos\theta$$

where **θ** is the instantaneous shaft angle.

These two signals are fed to a **Resolver-to-Digital (R/D) converter**, which performs synchronous demodulation (multiplying by the carrier and low-pass filtering) to recover sin θ and cos θ, then computes:

$$\theta = \arctan\left(\frac{\sin\theta}{\cos\theta}\right)$$

The angle is held in a binary up/down counter and presented to the CNC controller as a 14- or 16-bit parallel word representing the shaft position.

**Block diagram of the resolver-based CNC feedback loop:**

```
[ CNC Controller ] → [ Servo Amplifier ] → [ Servo Motor ] → [ Spindle ]
       ↑                                                           ↓
       ← ← ← ← [ R/D Converter ] ← ← ← [ Resolver on Motor Shaft ] ←
```

*(A Mermaid version of this same loop is given in Section 4.1.)*

**Why Resolvers Are Preferred in CNC Spindles:**

1. **No electronics on the rotor** — survives 200 °C spindle-bearing temperatures.
2. **Immune to oil mist, coolant, chips and EMI** — no fragile glass disc.
3. **Absolute within one revolution** — gives commutation angle for the spindle drive.
4. **High reliability** — MTBF often > 100,000 hours.

---

**(b) Explain the working of a tool-length measurement system and a tool-wear monitoring system used in modern CNC machines. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Tool-Length Measurement System:**

Every tool in a CNC machine has a slight variation in its actual length, and this length also changes with tool wear. The CNC controller must know the **exact Z-offset** of every tool in the ATC magazine to position the cutter tip correctly.

**Working — Touch-trigger Probe Method (Renishaw OMP60):**

1. The probe is mounted on the machine table. It contains a kinematic touch-trigger that fires a precise signal when the stylus is deflected by **as little as 1 µm**.
2. The CNC moves each tool in the spindle slowly down (e.g., G91 G01 Z-50 F200) towards the probe.
3. On contact, the probe sends a **5 V pulse** to the controller via a hard-wired or optical (infra-red) link.
4. The controller records the **machine coordinate at the instant of contact** and computes the Z-offset: $\text{Offset} = \text{Probe Height} - \text{Machine Z at Contact}$.
5. The offset is automatically written into the tool-offset memory (H-register) of the controller.

**Working — Laser Tool Setter Method (Marposs, Blum):**

A laser beam is projected through a collimator; when the tool touches the laser line, it shadows a photodetector. The system measures the tool position to within **±1 µm** without mechanical contact, avoiding stylus wear.

**Tool-Wear Monitoring System:**

1. **Direct Method — Tool Probing Between Passes:** After every few cutting passes, the tool is automatically moved to a touch probe in the work envelope. The diameter is measured and compared to the nominal. If the wear exceeds a programmed threshold (e.g., 0.05 mm), the controller either:
   - Triggers a tool-change request (replaces the worn tool), or
   - Engages an **automatic tool-offset compensation** to correct the tool path.

2. **Indirect Method — Cutting Force / Power Monitoring:** A strain-gauge dynamometer or spindle motor current sensor monitors the cutting load. A sudden increase in force or current beyond a programmed limit indicates **tool wear, chip jam, or tool breakage**. The controller triggers an E-stop and/or compensates the feedrate adaptively (Adaptive Control — AC).

3. **Acoustic-Emission (AE) Method:** A piezoelectric AE sensor mounted on the tool holder detects the high-frequency (100 kHz – 1 MHz) stress waves generated by flank wear and chip formation. Spectral analysis of the AE RMS voltage reveals the onset of catastrophic wear.

4. **Vision Method:** An on-machine CCD camera captures the flank face of the tool and image-processing software estimates the wear land width (VB) in real time.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout):**
> When asked to "compare" or "explain" tool-length and tool-wear systems, students frequently **confuse the two** and write about tool-length measurement in the wear section, or vice-versa. Be very clear: **tool-length = static offset at start of cut; tool-wear = dynamic change during the cut**. Conflating them is the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks in this question.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] A **transducer** converts one form of energy to another; in CNC, the output is almost always electrical.
- [ ] **Active sensors** (piezoelectric, thermocouple) self-generate; **passive sensors** (LVDT, strain gauge) need excitation.
- [ ] **Incremental encoders** give pulse counts from a reference (index) point and need homing after every power-off.
- [ ] **Absolute encoders** give a unique N-bit code for every shaft angle; no homing needed; used on spindle C-axis.
- [ ] **Quadrature decoding multiplies pulse count by 4** (both edges of both channels).
- [ ] **Resolver** = rugged analog rotary transformer with sin θ and cos θ outputs; survives harsh spindle environments.
- [ ] **LVDT secondaries are in series-opposition** → output is **subtractive** (e_o = e_{s1} − e_{s2}).
- [ ] LVDT sensitivity **K** has units **V/mm**; core displacement must stay within **±L/2** of null for linearity.
- [ ] **Direct measuring systems** (linear glass scales) eliminate ball-screw errors; **indirect systems** (rotary encoders on motor) include them.
- [ ] **Following (lag) error** E = V_f / K_v; reduced to ~0 by the **integral term** in PI / PID control.
- [ ] **Tool-length probe** sets the Z-offset at job start; **tool-wear probe** dynamically measures diameter during cutting.
- [ ] **Thermocouples** on lead-screws feed **thermal error compensation** software, critical for sub-µm accuracy.
- [ ] **Cutting force dynamometer** (piezoelectric) enables **Adaptive Control** (AC) — feed overrides in real time.
- [ ] The PID law is: **u(t) = K_p ε + K_i ∫ε dt + K_d (dε/dt)**.
- [ ] For KTU board numericals: **always** convert feedrate to mm/s before applying E = V_f / K_v.
- [ ] For encoder frequency: **f = (n · N · m) / 60** Hz, where n = rpm, N = lines/rev, m = quadrature factor.

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Examination Strategy for this Topic:**
> 1. Memorize the **differences table** between direct and indirect measuring systems.
> 2. Be able to **derive** the LVDT output equation from first principles.
> 3. Practice at least **three numericals** on encoder resolution and following error.
> 4. Always include a **neat labelled sketch** in any 7- or 14-mark answer — it earns you 1–2 free marks even if your written explanation is incomplete.
